classification.title: Chapter 33. The amendment clarifies that certain provisions do not apply to non-profit incorporated cemeteries. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 1
(S. B. 147)
AN ACT to provide for the regulation of aeronautics within this
Commonwealth in the interest of public safety and of aero
nautic progress; to create and set up a body politic to be known
as the Kentucky Aeronautic Commission; to prescribe the
powers and duties of said Commission; to create the office of
Director of Aeronautics of Kentucky and to prescribe the
powers and duties of such Director; to continue in force
present laws permitting cities of certain classes and certain
Counties to acquire, construct, maintain and operate airports
and landing fields; to repeal all laws in conflict with this Act;
and in general to promote the progress of aviation in the
Commonwealth.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Common wealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Kentucky Aeronautic Commission created;
members, qualifications, terms.
There is hereby created a bi-partisan commission
composed of six (6) members which shall be a body
politic and corporate, and shall be known as the
"Kentucky Aeronautic Commission" and in such
name said commission shall have the power to sue
and be sued; contract and be contracted with;

2 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
acquire and sell real and personal property by con
tract purchase, gift, condemnation or otherwise, and
may do all things reasonable or necessary to effec
tively carry out its work and properly perform the
duties intended or required under this Act within
the limits of its resources.
The members of the Commission shall be appointed
by the Governor, who shall be an ex-officio member
of the Commission. One half of the members of
the Commission shall be of one political party and
one half of the other political party which at the
last preceding general election respectively cast the
highest and the next highest number of votes. The
terms of office of the six (6) members first taking
office after the date of enactment of this Act shall
expire as designated by the Governor at the time of
appointment, two at the end of the first year; two at
the end of the second year; two at the end of the
third year. The two members whose terms expire
at the end of each year shall be members of op
posing political parties as hereinbefore provided.
If the Governor so elects, members may succeed
themselves. The terms of office of their successors
shall expire four years after the expiration of the
terms of their predecessors, except in appointments
to fill unexpired terms when there may be a vacancy,
in which case such appointment shall be for the
unexpired term of the predecessor. Each member
shall serve, except in case of resignation, until his
successor shall have been appointed and qualified.
Each member shall be white and at least twenty-five
years of age and at least two of such members shall
be, without respect to their political affiliations,
actively engaged in, affiliated with or identified
with aviation.
§ 2. Compensation; officers; rules and regu
lations.

of a misdemeanor and upon conviction shall be
punished by a fine of not more than one hundred
($100.00) dollars or by imprisonment for not more

any and all funds collected under the provisions of

made unlawful by this Act shall not be deemed to
include any act or omission which violates the laws
or lawful regulations of the United States, but it
shall not be necessary to allege or prove, as part of

§ 4. The Kentucky Agricultural Experiment
Station shall not be financially responsible for any
debts incurred by any damages inflicted by, or any

§ 5. The Kentucky Agricultural Experiment
Station, or its designated agency or agencies, may
withhold certification for a period of two or more
years from any grower of seeds or plant parts who
is engaged in or attempting to engage in any dis
honest practices for the purpose of evading the
provisions of this Act, including standards, rules
and regulations laid down by the Kentucky Agri
cultural Experiment Station, or its duly authorized
agency, to cover certification.
§ 6. It shall be unlawful for any person, firm,
association, or corporation to issue, make, use or
circulate any certification, as defined in this act,
without the authority and approval of the Kentucky
Agricultural Experiment Station, or its duly
authorized agency, as herein provided. Every
person, firm, association, or corporation who shall
violate any of the provisions of this Act pertaining
to certification shall be deemed guilty of a mis
demeanor, and upon conviction thereof shall be
fined in any sum not less than one hundred dollars
($100.00) nor exceeding five hundred dollars
($500.00) for each offense.
§ 7. The duty of enforcing this Act and carrying
out its provisions and requirements shall be vested
in the Director of the Kentucky Agricultural
Experiment Station or his agents.

nor February 7, 1938, is hereby repealed, amended
and re-enacted so that when so amended and re-
enacted it shall provide as follows :
"Section Two. The Kentucky State Fair Board
shall be composed of nine members designated and
appointed as follows :
(1) The Governor of the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky ;
(2) The Commissioner of Agriculture, Labor
and Statistics;
(3) The Dean of the College of Agriculture of
the University of Kentucky;
(4) Six members to be appointed from the State
at large by the Governor for terms as follows:
The six members initially appointed shall hold
their offices respectively, as designated by the
Governor, two for a term of one year, two for a
term of two years, and two for a term of three years,
all such terms to be treated as if beginning con
temporaneously with the Governor's term of office.
Upon the expiration of the respective terms of such

(H. B. 80)
AN ACT to declare the necessity of creating governmental sub
divisions of the State, to be known as "soil conservation dis
tricts," to engage in conserving soil resources and preventing
and controlling soil erosion; to establish the State soil conser
vation committee, and to define its powers and duties; to pro
vide for the creation of soil conservation districts; to define
the powers and duties of soil conservation districts, and to
provide for the exercise of such powers, including the power
to acquire property by purchase, gift, and otherwise; to em
power such districts to adopt programs and regulations for the
discontinuance of land-use practices contributing to soil
wastage and soil erosion, an'd the adoption and carrying out of
soil-conserving land-use practices, and to provide for the
enforcement of such programs and regulations; to provide for
establishing boards of adjustment in connection with land-use
regulations, and to define their functions and powers; to pro
vide for the discontinuance of soil conservation districts; and
for other purposes. This Act shall be known and cited as
"The Robinson, Vance and Blake Act".
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Short Title. —This act may be known and
cited as the soil conservation districts law.

38 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
§ 2. Legislative Determinations, and Declara
tion of Policy. —It is hereby declared, as a matter of
legislative determination —
A. The condition. —That the farm and grazing
lands of the Commonwealth of Kentucky are among
the basic assets of the Commonwealth of Kentucky,
and that the preservation of these lands is necessary
to protect and promote the health, safety, and
general welfare of its people; that improper land-
use practices have caused and have contributed to,
and are now causing and contributing to, a pro
gressively more serious erosion of the farm and
grazing lands of this Commonwealth by wind and
water; that the breaking of natural grass, plant,
and forest cover have interfered with the natural
factors of soil stabilization, causing loosening of
soil and exhaustion of humus, and developing a
soil condition that favors erosion; that the topsoil
is being blown and washed out of fields and
pastures ; that there has been an accelerated washing
of sloping fields ; that these processes of erosion by
wind and water speed up with removal of absorptive
topsoil, causing exposure of less absorptive and
less protective but more erosive subsoil ; that failure
by any land occupier to conserve the soil and control
erosion upon his lands causes a washing and blow
ing of soil and water from his lands onto other
lands and makes the conservation of soil and control
of erosion on such other lands difficult or impos
sible.
B. The consequences. —That the consequences
of such soil erosion in the form of soil-blowing and
soil-washing are the silting and sedimentation of
stream channels, reservoirs, dams, ditches, and
harbors; the loss of fertile soil material in dust
storms ; the piling up of soil on lower slopes, and its
deposit over alluvial plains; the reduction in pro
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 85

classification.summary: This section of the 1940 Acts of the General Assembly declares that farm and grazing lands are basic assets of Kentucky and that their preservation is necessary for public health, safety, and welfare. It details the problem of soil erosion caused by improper land-use practices, including the breaking of natural cover, leading to soil loss and sedimentation of waterways. The statute emphasizes that failure to conserve soil on one's land negatively impacts neighboring lands.

CHAPTER 8 39
ductivity or outright ruin of rich bottom lands by
overwash of poor subsoil material, sand, and gravel
swept out of the hills; deterioration of soil and its
fertility, deterioration of crops grown thereon, and
declining acre yields despite development of
scientific processes for increasing such yields; loss
of soil and water which causes destruction of food
and cover for wildlife; a blowing and washing of
soil into streams which silts over spawning beds,
and destroys water plants, diminishing the food
supply of fish; a diminishing of the underground
water reserve, which causes water shortages, in
tensifies periods of drought, and causes crop
failures; an increase in the speed and volume of
rainfall runoff, causing severe and increasing
floods, which bring suffering, disease, and death;
impoverishment of families attempting to farm
eroding and eroded lands; damage to roads, high
ways, railways, farm buildings, and other property
from floods and from dust storms; and losses in
navigation, hydroelectric power, municipal water
supply, irrigation developments, farming, and
grazing.
C. The appropriate corrective methods. —That
to conserve soil resources and control and prevent
soil erosion, it is necessary that land-use practices
contributing to soil wastage and soil erosion be dis
couraged and discontinued, and appropriate soil-
conserving land-use practices be adopted and
carried out; that among the procedures necessary
for widespread adoption, are the carrying on of
engineering operations such as the construction of
terraces, terrace outlets, check-dams, dikes, ponds,
ditches, and the like; the utilization of strip crop
ping, lister furrowing, contour cultivating, and con
tour furrowing; land irrigation; seeding and plant
ing of waste, sloping, abandoned, or eroded lands

to water-conserving and erosion-preventing plants,
trees, and grasses; forestation and reforestation;

ing crops; retardation of runoff by increasing

D. Declaration of policy. —It is hereby declared
to be the policy of the legislature to provide for the
conservation of the soil and soil resources of this
Commonwealth, and for the control and prevention
of soil erosion, and thereby to preserve natural
resources, control floods, prevent impairment of
dams and reservoirs, assist in maintaining the navi
gability of rivers and harbors, preserve wildlife,
protect the tax base, protect public lands, and pro
tect and promote the health, safety, and general wel
fare of the people of this Commonwealth.
§ 3. Definitions.—Wherever used or referred to
in this act, unless a different meaning clearly appears
from the context :
(1) "District" or "soil conservation district"
means a governmental subdivision of this Common
wealth, and a public body corporate and politic,
organized in accordance with the provisions of this
act, for the purposes, with the powers, and subject
to the restrictions hereinafter set forth.
(2) "Supervisor" means one of the members of
the governing body of a district, elected or appointed
in accordance with the provisions of this act.
(3) "Committee" or "State soil conservation

CHAPTER 8 23
§ 6. Election of Three Supervisors for Each
District.— Within thirty (30) days after the date of
issuance by the secretary of state of a certificate of
organization of a soil conservation district, nominat
ing petitions may be filed with the State soil conser
vation committee to nominate candidates for super
visors of such district. The committee shall have
authority to extend the time within which nominat
ing petitions may be filed. No such nominating
petition shall be accepted by the committee,
unless it shall be subscribed by twenty-five (25) or
more owners of lands lying within the boundaries of
such district. Land owners may sign more than one
such nominating petition to nominate more than one
candidate for supervisor. The committee shall give
due notice of an election to be held for the election of
three supervisors for the district. The names of all
nominees on behalf of whom such nominating peti
tions have been filed within the time herein desig
nated, shall be printed, arranged in the alphabetical
order of the surnames, upon ballots, with a square
before each name and a direction to insert an X mark
in the square before any three names to indicate the
voter 's preference. All owners of lands lying within
the district shall be eligible to vote in such election.
Only such land owners shall be eligible to vote. The
three candidates who shall receive the largest num
ber, respectively, of the votes cast in such election
shall be the elected supervisors for such district.
The committee shall pay all the expenses of such
election, shall supervise the conduct thereof, shall
prescribe regulations governing the conduct of such
election and the determination of the eligibility of
voters therein, and shall publish the results thereof.
§ 7. Appointment, Qualifications and Tenure of
Supervisors. — The governing body of the district
shall consist of five (5) supervisors, elected or ap
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 100

adoption of land-use regulations. Referenda on
adoption, amendment, supplementation, or repeal
of land-use regulations shall not be held more often
than once in six (6) months.
The regulations to be adopted by the supervisors
under the provisions of this section may include :
1. Provisions requiring the carrying out of
necessary engineering operations, including the
construction of terraces, terrace outlets, check
dams, dikes, ponds, ditches, and other necessary
structures ;
2. Provisions requiring observance of particular
methods of cultivation including contour cultivating,
contour furrowing, lister furrowing, sowing,
planting, strip cropping, seeding, and planting of
lands to water-conserving and erosion-preventing
plants, trees and grasses, forestation, and re
forestation ;
3. Specifications of cropping programs and
tillage practices to be observed;
4. Provisions requiring the retirement from
cultivation of highly erosive areas or of areas on
which erosion may not be adequately controlled if
cultivation is carried on;
5. Provisions for such other means, measures,
operations, and programs as may assist conserva
tion of soil resources and prevent or control soil
erosion in the district, having due regard to the
legislative findings set forth in section 2 of this act.
The regulations shall be uniform throughout the
territory comprised within the district except that
the supervisors may classify the lands within the
district with reference to such factors as soil type,
degree of slope, degree of erosion threatened or
existing, cropping and tillage practices in use, and
other relevant factors, and may provide regula
tions varying with the type or class of land affected,

It details the process for supervisors to file petitions for costs and expenses incurred in completing work ordered by the court, and establishes a Board of Adjustment consisting of three members appointed by the State soil conservation committee with the advice of district supervisors. The board members serve three-year terms and can be removed for neglect of duty or malfeasance in office after notice and hearing.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text discusses the organization and operation of soil conservation districts, including the establishment of a Board of Adjustment and procedures for recovering costs. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1940. The confidence is high because the content is clearly unrelated to racial discrimination. The category is 'other' as it pertains to land use and environmental regulation, not the typical categories associated with Jim Crow laws.

classification.title: An Act relating to soil conservation districts and boards of adjustment.
classification.summary: This act outlines the procedures and compensation for members of a board of adjustment within soil conservation districts. It details their ineligibility for appointment if they hold other offices, their per diem and expense compensation, the adoption of rules, the designation of a chairman, meeting procedures, quorum requirements, the administration of oaths, and the public nature of meetings and records. It also allows landowners or occupiers to file petitions with the board regarding difficulties in complying with land-use regulations.

72 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 9.
(H. B. 90)
AN ACT to bond weighmen in stockyards, to provide for monthly
reports from stockyards, to place supervision of stockyards and
weighing devices therein in the Department of Agriculture,
Labor and Statistics, and to provide for inspection of weighing
devices.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Definitions as used in this Act unless the con
tent requires otherwise: (1) "Department" means
the Department of Agriculture, Labor and Statistics.
(2) "Stock yards" means livestock yards, market
places, concentration points, packing plants or any
other place where livestock is bought and sold, other
than one which is posted and subject to regulation
by the Secretary of Agriculture of the United States
under the Packers and Stock Yards Act, 1921, and
laws amendatory thereof and the rules and regula
tions promulgated thereunder by said Secretary.
§ 2. Weighmen to be bonded: All stock yards
employing weighmen shall submit to the Department
a list of the names of all such weighmen employed.
Each weighman so listed shall furnish a bond in the
amount of One Thousand Dollars to the Department
for the faithful performance of duty and should any
weighman knowingly and willfully misrepresent or
attempt to misrepresent the weight of any animal or
animals for the purpose of misleading the public
such bond shall be forfeited and the proceeds thereof
paid into the State Treasury to be credited to the
General Fund.
§ 3. Only bonded weighmen allowed to weigh
livestock: No person other than the bonded weigh
man, as provided in Section 2 of this Act, shall be
permitted to weigh livestock for the purpose of sale
at any stock yard.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 119

classification.title: AN ACT to bond weighmen in stockyards, to provide for monthly reports from stockyards, to place supervision of stockyards and weighing devices therein in the Department of Agriculture, Labor and Statistics, and to provide for inspection of weighing devices.
classification.summary: This act requires weighmen in stockyards to be bonded and prohibits anyone other than a bonded weighman from weighing livestock for sale. It also mandates monthly reports from stockyards and places supervision of stockyards and weighing devices under the Department of Agriculture, Labor and Statistics.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of weighmen and stockyards, focusing on financial accountability and operational oversight. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The regulations apply to all weighmen and stockyards regardless of the race of the individuals involved or the livestock being weighed. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 10.
(H. B. 115)
AN ACT creating a division of Markets in the Department of
Agriculture, Labor and Statistics, defining its powers, and
declaring an emergency to exist.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. There is hereby created a Division of Mar
kets in the Department of Agriculture, Labor and
Statistics. The Division shall be under the super
vision of the Commissioner of Agriculture, Labor
and Statistics, and shall consist of such personnel as
is determined and appointed 'by the Commissioner,
provided that such appointments shall be subject to
the provisions of the Reorganization Act of 1936.
The Division shall be headed by a director, who
shall receive a salary not exceeding $3,900.00 annual
ly, to be appointed by the Commissioner of Agricul
ture, Labor and Statistics, subject to the approval
of the Governor.
§ 2. The Division of Markets shall have the fol
lowing powers and duties :
(a) To stress promotional market efforts and
make available to farmers practical assistance in set
ting up farm cooperatives ;
(b) To gather and disseminate information con
cerning supply, demand, prevailing price, and com
mercial movement, including common and cold stor
age of food products, and maintain market news
service for the purpose of disseminating such infor
mation ;
(c) To foster and encourage the inspection, grad
ing, standardizing, labelling and branding of farm
products, to provide standards of excellence and
brands for the use of producers and consumers in

CHAPTER 11.
(H. B. 209)
AN ACT to establish a Kentucky Farm Tenancy Commission; to
define its work; to provide for its conduct and expenses; and
for other purposes.
WHEREAS, more than a third of Kentucky farm families are
tenants or croppers; and,
WHEREAS, such tenancy appears to be increasing for the state
and nation; and,
WHEREAS, soil erosion and exhaustion, low income and poverty,
low health standards, illiteracy, decadence of the rural church
and of the social life of the community usually follows; and,
WHEREAS, thoughtful and capable citizens, including landlords
and farm tenants, have expressed their belief that such prob
lems should be studied and means sought for their solution;
and,
WHEREAS, it is vital to the long-time welfare of the farm people,
rural communities, and the state and nation, that ability, thrift,
and the other necessary characteristics incident to attaining
land ownership and improving tenancy be recognized; now
therefore
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. There is hereby established a Kentucky
Farm Tenancy Commission, to be composed of nine
(9) members appointed by the Governor, one of
whom shall be a bona fide resident of each congres
sional district. The members of the Commission
shall receive no compensation for their services, but
from the appropriation of one thousand dollars

82 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 12
(H. B. 407)
AN ACT to provide for the supervision, regulation and licensing
of tobacco warehouses and for the bonding of weighmen in
tobacco warehouses and authorizing the Commissioner of
Agriculture to conduct an investigation in selling costs of
tobacco.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Definitions.—As used in this Act unless the
content requires otherwise. (1) "Department"
means the Department of Agriculture, Labor and
Statistics. '(2) "Warehouse" means any tobacco
Warehouse operating as a place of sale for tobacco
in the Commonwealth. (3) "Warehousemen"
means the owner, operator, manager, lessee or
proprietor of a warehouse engaged in selling
tobacco or their agents or employees. (4) "Com
missioner" means the Commissioner of Agriculture,
Labor and Statistics. (5) "Grower" means a bona
fide producer of tobacco, either by himself or by
tenant or share-cropper or hired person.
§ 2. Weighmen to be Bonded. —Warehouses
operating within the confines of the Commonwealth
shall submit to the Department a list of the names
of all weighmen employed. Each weighman so
listed shall furnish a bond in the amount of $500.00
to the Department for the faithful performance of
duty and should any weighman knowingly and
willfully misrepresent or attempt to misrepresent
the weight of any tobacco weighed by him on the
Warehouse floor for the purpose of misleading the
public, such bond shall be forfeited and the proceeds
thereof paid into the State Treasury to be credited
to the General Fund.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 129

CHAPTER 12 83
§ 3. Only bonded Weighmen Allowed to Weigh
Tobacco. —No person other than the bonded weigh-
men, as provided in Section Two of this Act, shall
be permitted to weigh tobacco for the purpose of
sale at any tobacco warehouse.
§ 4. Height of Basket and Weight of Tobacco
Limited. —Baskets of tobacco when placed on ware
house floors for the purpose of sale shall not exceed
five feet in height and each basket shall contain not
more than seven hundred pounds of tobacco.
§ 5. N-umber of Baskets Sold Limited and Ware
house Operating Day and Week Set. — The maximum
rate of sales of baskets of tobacco on warehouse
floors shall not exceed a rate of 360 baskets per
hour; nor shall more than 360 baskets be sold during
any one hour. The operating day at every ware
house operating within the Commonwealth shall be
limited to five actual selling hours and the operat
ing week of every warehouse shall be limited to
five actual selling days.
§ 6. Sales of Vocational Education Tobacco. —
In addition to the days allowed for sales under
section 6, each warehouse may operate one Saturday
during- each season for the sale of tobacco grown
by persons under twenty-one years of age under a
vocational education program sponsored by
county agents, or other county, state or federal
agencies. Section 11 shall not apply to such sales.
§ 7. Discrimination Prohibited. — No warehouse
or warehouseman shall discriminate against any
grower of tobacco by failing to accord to such
grower or growers all privileges and services ex
tended to any other grower, nor shall any ware
house or warehouseman refuse to admit any tobacco
to the warehouse floor so long as there is space
available or reserved space for the placing of said
tobacco upon the floor for the purpose of sale.

§ 8. Involuntary Commingling of Tobacco Pro
hibited. —The commingling of crops of tobacco on
the Warehouse floor while such tobacco is still the
property of the grower shall be prohibited unless
express permission for such commingling is obtained
from the grower.
§ 9. Rebates Prohibited. —No warehouse or
warehouseman shall give a rebate of any part of the

as an inducement for the purpose of influencing the

criminating against any grower or growers, nor

CHAPTER 12 85

§ 11. Department to Enforce Act and Employ
Inspectors. — (1) The Department shall administer
this Act and shall have general supervision over
the sale of tobacco in the various warehouses
throughout the Commonwealth.
(2) The Commissioner shall employ such in
spectors and other employees as are necessary for
the enforcement of this Act and the duties and com
pensation of such employees shall be fixed by the
Commissioner in accordance with the provisions of
the governmental reorganization act of 1936.
§ 12. Duties of Inspectors.— (1) The inspectors
employed under the provisions of Section Thirteen
of this Act, shall carry out a general inspection
service of all warehouses in the state and it shall
be the duty of such inspectors to strictly enforce
the provisions of this Act.
(2) The inspectors employed by the Department
shall inspect the weights, measures, weighing
devices and measuring devices of all tobacco ware
houses and shall stamp such weights, measures,
weighing devices and measuring devices with a
mark which shall be placed in a conspicuous position
on the device inspected and such stamp shall certify
that such device has been inspected by an agent
of the Department and that such device has been
found to comply strictly with the laws of the State
and of the United States and is in every sense
exactly what it purports to be.
(3) Inspectors employed by the Department
shall have the privilege of reweighing any tobacco
at any warehouse at any time that such inspector
is reasonably certain that such tobacco has been
improperly weighed.

CHAPTER 12 87
§ 14. Commissioner to Conduct Investigation
Into Selling Cost. — The Commissioner shall con
duct an investigation during the years 1940 and
1941 for the purpose of determining the exact cost
to the warehouse of selling tobacco in the various
warehouses operating in the Commonwealth and
shall submit a report covering the data so col
lected to the next regular session of the Legislature
following the enactment of this law or to any agency
of the said Legislature appointed for the purpose of
receiving said report. The report of the Commis
sioner in addition to including the actual cost of
sales to the warehouses in each marketing center
within the State shall include the charges made by
such warehouses to the growers in each of these
centers during the years covered by the investiga
tion. The Commissioner shall have the right to
promulgate rules and regulations as to the manner
in which such investigation shall be made and to
appoint agents for the purpose of carrying out this
investigation.
§ 15. Penalties. — (1) Any person who violates
Section Two of this Act or who violates Section
Three of this Act or any owner, lessee, manager or
operator of any tobacco warehouse who attempts
by intimidation, force, bribery or by any other means
to influence any weighman to falsely report the
weight of any tobacco sold on the floors of said
warehouse shall be fined not less than Five Hundred
Dollars nor more than One Thousand Dollars.
(2) Any person who violates Section Four of
this Act or who violates Section Five of this Act
or who violates Section Six of this Act or who
violates Section Ten of this Act or who violates
Section Twelve of this Act or who in any way
impersonates, hinders or obstructs the department
or its inspectors in the performance of their officia I

entries.ocr_text:
CHAPTER 12 89
The bill as passed by the House of Representatives contained
the following section :
RESERVATION OF FLOOR SPACE PROHIBITED. No ware
house or warehouseman shall reserve any floor space for any
producer or consignor of tobacco, nor shall he discriminate
against any grower of tobacco by failing to accord to such grower
or growers all privileges and services extended to any other
grower, nor shall any warehouse or warehouseman refuse to
admit any tobacco to the warehouse floor so long as there is space
available for the placing of said tobacco upon the floor for the
purpose of sale.
It was evidently the intent of the Senate to amend the bill so
as to remove the inhibition against reserving of floor space and
permit this practice to continue. This is indicated by the fact
that there was adopted an amendment offered by the Rules Com
mittee and concurred in by the House which is as follows:
Strike the words "reserve any floor space for any producer or
consignor of tobacco nor shall he" and by striking title of section
"Reservation of Floor Space Prohibited" and insert words "Dis
crimination Prohibited."
Another amendment adopted was as follows:
Amend H. B. 407 by inserting in line 7 of old Section 8, now
Section 7, after the word "available" and before the word "for",
the following words "or reserved space."
It is obvious that the last amendment meant to specify "or
unreserved space" in order to be in harmony with the first amend
ment. Since through inadvertance this error occurred, the mean
ing is not clear. But since the intent of the Legislature was
indicated in the major amendment as desiring to remove from
the bill that portion of it which would have made it unlawful to
reserve floor space on tobacco warehouses, such will be con
struction placed on the bill as it is administered.
The amended section as enacted reads as follows:
DISCRIMINATION PROHIBITED. No warehouse or ware
houseman shall discriminate against any grower of tobacco by
failing to accord to such grower or growers all privileges and
services extended to any other grower, nor shall any warehouse
or warehouseman refuse to admit any tobacco to the warehouse
floor so long as there is space available or reserved space for
the placing of said tobacco upon the floor for the purpose of sale.
Approved by the Governor, March 26, 1940.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 136

classification.title: DISCRIMINATION PROHIBITED
classification.summary: This statute, originally intended to prohibit warehouses from reserving floor space for tobacco producers and from discriminating against growers, was amended. The amendments removed the prohibition on reserving floor space and clarified that discrimination is prohibited, meaning warehouses cannot refuse to admit tobacco if space is available or reserved. The focus is on fair treatment and access for all tobacco growers.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses discrimination in the tobacco warehousing industry. While it prohibits discrimination and ensures fair access to warehouse space for all growers, it does not contain any explicit or implicit racial components. The amendments focus on the business practice of reserving floor space and ensuring equal treatment for all producers, regardless of their race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an act passed by the General Assembly in 1940. The confidence is high because the text clearly indicates the statute's focus on business practices rather than racial segregation.

90 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 13
H. B. 276
AN ACT called "DISTILLED SPIRITS AND WINE FAIR TRADE
ACT", providing for fair trade contracts with minimum resale
prices from resident producers, processors and vintners to
wholesalers; from wholesalers and importers to retailers; from
non-resident producers, processors and vintners, on brands sold
in this State to wholesalers and importers; from importers to
retailers; providing for amendments as to change in price; pro
visions regulating close out sales; registration of brands by
resident producers, processors, and vintners; registration of
brands by non-resident producers, processors, vintners and
wholesalers ; enumerating the purposes of the Act; setting out
the powers of the Board; providing penalties for the violation
of any rule or regulation made by the Board for the enforce
ment of this Act; providing penalties for the violation of any
provision of this Act; providing for the levy, collection and dis
tribution of revenue for the enforcement of this Act; defining
"words and phrases" used in this Act and in Fair Trade Act
(Ky. 1936-37 Acts, Fourth Special Session, Chapter 24) ; provid
ing that if any part of this Act shall be held invalid and uncon
stitutional this shall not affect the validity of any remaining
part of this Act; and declaring an emergency to exist.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Reference to Alcoholic Beverage Control
Laiv of 1938. —Except as otherwise herein specif
ically provided this Act shall be administered in the
manner provided for the regulation and super
vision of licensees authorized under the Alcoholic
Beverage Control Law of 1938 and for the purpose
of the administration, construction and interpreta
tion of the provisions of this Act, the 1938 Alcoholic
Beverage Control Law, being Chapter 2 of the
Acts of the 1938 General Assembly, now compiled
as Sections 2554b-98 to 2554b-222, inclusive, of
Baldwin's 1938 Supplement of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes and any and all amendments thereto, be
and the same is hereby referred to and adopted.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 137

classification.title: DISTILLED SPIRITS AND WINE FAIR TRADE ACT
classification.summary: This act establishes a fair trade system for distilled spirits and wine in Kentucky, setting minimum resale prices for various levels of distribution (producers to wholesalers, wholesalers to retailers, etc.). It also outlines provisions for price amendments, close-out sales, brand registration, and the powers of the Board. The act is to be administered under the Alcoholic Beverage Control Law of 1938.

Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

duced others,' shall offer same for sale in this

All distilled spirits or wines sold in this State

as defined in Section 1 of the Fair Trade Act, (Ky.

following minimum resale prices without discount,

wholesalers of not less than 20%.

imum mark-up resale price without discount for
retailers of not less than 33-1/3% in less than case
lots and not less than 10% in units of one case or
more.

bates of any character whatsoever which constitute
any part of an inducement to purchase, before or

classification.title: An Act relating to the sale of stocks of goods.
classification.summary: This act outlines the conditions and procedures for conducting close-out sales of stocks of goods in Kentucky. It requires that the goods have been in the state for at least three months and were not brought in specifically for a close-out sale. Additionally, the goods must have been first offered to the original producer, wholesaler, or vendor at the invoice price or less, with their refusal to repurchase being a prerequisite. The Department of Alcoholic Beverage Control has the discretion to allow or refuse such sales, with specific procedural remedies for licensees if a sale is refused.

classification.title: CHAPTER 13 97
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for registering brands of distilled spirits and wines for sale in Kentucky. It mandates that both resident and non-resident producers, processors, vintners, and wholesalers must register their brands and specify the licensed wholesalers to whom distributing rights have been granted. It also prohibits licensed wholesalers from obtaining an importer's permit or importing/selling brands from non-resident entities until these distribution rights are properly granted and registered.

unconstitutional such decision shall not effect the
validity of the remaining parts hereof.
§ 14. Declaring an Emergency.—This is an Act
to produce revenue and to assist in the proper
enforcement of the laws governing the Distilled
Spirits and Wine Industries, and in order to provide
the funds necessary for the proper enforcement of
said Act, it is necessary that said Act become im
mediately effective, therefore it is now provided
that in respect hereto, an emergency exists and this
Act shall become a law and be effective on its
passage and approval by the Governor, or shall
become a law without his approval.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Repeal, amend and re-enact Section 17 of
Chapter 2 of the Acts of the General Assembly of
1938, so that when amended and re-enacted it shall
read as follows :
City Licenses and Taxes ; Provisions Concerning.
The city council, board of aldermen, or other munic
ipal legislative body of each incorporated city in
this Commonwealth in which traffic in alcoholic
beverages is permitted under sections 2554c-1 to
2554c-42 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edi

collect license fees or taxes for the privilege of
engaging in the business of trafficking in alcoholic
beverages. It shall be the duty of the city clerk
immediately to notify the State Alcoholic Beverage
Control Board of the amount of the taxes fixed.
Only such licenses may be issued as correspond in
their provisions and the business authorized, to the

of the General ASsembly of 1938, and subsections
six and seven of Section 29, Chapter 2 of the Acts
of the General ASsembly of 1938, and subsections
one, two, three and six of Section 96 of Chapter 2
of the Acts of the General ASsembly of 1938. The
license fees or taxes imposed shall in no event exceed
twice the amount of the fees or taxes imposed in sec
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 149

classification.title: AN ACT relating to alcoholic beverages, repealing, amending, and re-enacting Section 17 of Chapter 2 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1938; and Sections 1 and 2 of Chapter 5 of the Acts of the General Assembly at the 1st Extraordinary Session of 1938.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 17 of Chapter 2 of the 1938 Acts of the General Assembly of Kentucky. It grants city legislative bodies the power to impose and collect license fees or taxes for the privilege of trafficking in alcoholic beverages, provided that such traffic is permitted under specific sections of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes. The city clerk is required to notify the State Alcoholic Beverage Control Board of the tax amounts. The act also specifies that only licenses corresponding to certain subsections of previous acts may be issued, and that license fees or taxes cannot exceed twice the amount of fees or taxes previously imposed.

It grants authority to city councils to set license fees and requires notification to the State Alcoholic Beverage Control Board. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not classified as a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the chapter numbering. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on licensing and taxation of a business, with no discernible racial component.

classification.title: An Act relating to the sale of distilled spirits and wine and malt beverages.
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for the sale of distilled spirits, wine, and malt beverages in Kentucky. It grants cities of the first, second, and third classes the exclusive right to set hours for sales within their limits. It also gives fiscal courts the power to set hours for areas outside these city limits. Specific restrictions include no sales between 6 a.m. Sunday and 6 a.m. Monday, and no sales on election days while polls are open. Brewers and distributors are prohibited from delivering malt beverages between 7:00 P.M. and 6:00 A.M. on weekdays, with different Saturday hours, and no Sunday deliveries. Beer retailers are also restricted from selling, giving away, or delivering malt beverages between midnight and a specified morning hour (though the text is cut off). All liquor stocks must be kept locked during prohibited sales hours.
classification.reasoning: The statute regulates the sale and delivery hours of alcoholic beverages (distilled spirits, wine, and malt beverages) at the state and local levels. It outlines restrictions based on time of day, day of the week (Sunday, election days), and location (within or outside city limits). There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The regulations appear to be general public policy concerning the sale of alcohol. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is an 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' from 1940. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on alcohol sales regulations and lacks any racial component. The category is 'other' as it does not fit into the specific categories provided for Jim Crow laws.

tucky, may apply to the Director for permission to
organize a credit union. The term "The Director"
as used in this Act shall mean the Director of the

Regulation of the Commonwealth of Kentucky. Per
sons uniting to form a credit union shall sign and
acknowledge in duplicate articles of incorporation
and shall enter into articles of agreement, in which
they shall bind themselves to comply with its require
ments and with all the laws, rules and regulations
applicable to credit unions. The certificate of
organization shall contain the following :
(1) The name of the proposed credit union.
(2) A statement that incorporation is desired
under this particular law.
(3) The par value of shares, which shall be five
dollars.
(4) The city, town or village in which the business
office of the corporation is to be located.
(5) The names and addresses of the subscribers
to the articles of incorporation and a statement of
the number of shares of stock subscribed by each.
At the time of filing the said articles of incorpora
tion with the Director the incorporators shall sub
mit, in duplicate, a set of by-laws (with acknowledg
ment of their adoption by the incorporators) which
shall provide :
(a) The date of the annual meeting, which shall
be in January of each year; the manner in which
members shall be notified of meetings ; the manner of
conducting meetings; the number of members con
stituting a quorum with regulations as to voting.
(b) The number of directors, which shall not be
less than five, all of whom shall be members of the
corporation; their powers and duties, together with
the duties of officers elected by the board of directors.

classification.title: CHAPTER 20 197
classification.summary: This statute outlines limitations on the indebtedness and obligations of banks. It specifies that a bank cannot act as a guarantor or surety for an entity exceeding ten percent of its paid-up capital stock without securing the excess with property double its value. Furthermore, the total indebtedness of any person, company, or firm, including individual members, cannot exceed thirty percent of the bank's paid-up capital and actual surplus. Loans made for the benefit of a person, company, or firm are included in their total liabilities. Exceptions are made for the discount of bills of exchange drawn against existing value and the purchase or discounting of commercial paper owned by the negotiating party. The statute also exempts obligations of the United States, the State of Kentucky, and entities guaranteed by them, as well as obligations of Kentucky counties and school districts incurred in anticipation of current year's tax receipts.

§ 6. Condemnation authorized; proceedings. —
Whenever it shall become necessary to condemn
property for the purpose of constructing any low
cost housing project, or securing rights-of-way
leading thereto, the Commission may condemn any
interest, franchises, easements, rights, or privileges,
land or improvements, which may in its Opinion,
be necessary for the purpose of constructing said
low cost housing project.
The Commission may by resolution reciting such
need, order the condemnation of such property, and
proceedings for such condemnation shall be in the
circuit court of the county in which the property
lies, and shall be conducted in the name of the
Commission by the County Attorney of the county,
and the judgment of the court shall vest title to the
property condemned in the Commission. In all
other respects, the form and manner of the pro
ceedings shall be the same as that provided for the
condemnation of property by boards of park com
missioners in Section 2852 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes. No payment or award in any condemna
tion proceedings, or for the cost of such proceed
ings, or the expense thereof shall be made except
from funds provided under the authority of this
Act.

§ 15. Commission may contract to manage proj
ects of other governmental agencies. —A Housing
Commission shall have the pwer by contract with
the owners thereof, to manage and/or operate any
low cost housing project erected and/or owned by
the United States Housing Authority or any other
Governmental Agency.
§ 16. Co-operation between Housing Commis
sions. —Any two or more Housing Commissions may
join or co-operate with one another in the exercise
of any or all of the powers conferred on such
Commissions for the purpose of financing, planning,
undertaking, constructing or operating a housing
project or projects located within the area of opera
tion of any one or more of such Commissions.
§ 17. Short title. —This Act may be referred to
as the "Rural Housing Commission Act."
§ 18. Supplemental Nature of Act. —The powers
conferred by this Act shall be in addition and sup
plemental to the powers conferred by any other
law.

CHAPTER 24.

AN ACT to authorize cities, towns, villages, counties and other

States of America by conveying or dedicating property, by

CHAPTER 24 221
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Short Title. — This Act may be referred to as
the "Housing Cooperation Act."
§ 2. Finding and Declaration of Necessity.—It is
hereby declared that there exist in, the Common
wealth unsafe and insanitary housing conditions and
a shortage of safe and sanitary dwelling accommoda
tions for persons of low income; that these condi
tions necessitate excessive and disproportionate
expenditures of public funds for crime prevention
and punishment, public health and safety, fire and
accident protection, and other public services and
facilities; and that the public interest requires the
remedying of these' conditions. It is hereby further
declared that the assistance herein provided for the
remedying of such conditions constitutes a public
use and purpose and an essential governmental func
tion for which public moneys may be spent, and other
aid given ; that it is a proper public purpose for any
Public Body to aid any Housing Commission operat
ing within its boundaries or jurisdiction or any hous
ing project located therein, as the Public Body
derives immediate benefits and advantages from such
a Housing Commission or project ; and that the pro
visions hereinafter enacted are necessary in the
public interest.
§ 3. Definitions. — The following terms, whenever
used or referred to in this Act shall have the follow
ing respective meanings, unless a different meaning
clearly appears from the context:
(a) "Housing Commission" shall mean any Hous
ing Commission created pursuant to the Municipal
Housing Commission Act or the Rural Housing Com
mission Act.

city, town or county located in whole or in part with

donate money to such Housing Commission or to

granted may be authorized by resolution or ordi
nance of the legislative body of such Public Body
adopted by a majority of the members of the legisla
tive body present at a meeting of said legislative
body, which resolution or ordinance may be adopted
at the meeting at which such resolution is introduced.
Such a resolution or ordinance shall take effect im
mediately and need not be laid over or published or
posted.

conferred by this Act shall be in addition and sup
plemental to the powers conferred by any other law.

der of the Act and the application of such provision

CHAPTER 26 229
providing decent, safe and sanitary dwelling accom
modations, and no Housing Commission shall con
struct or operate any such project for profit, or as a
source of revenue to the city. In the operation or
management of housing projects a Municipal Hous
ing Commission shall at all times observe the follow
ing duties with respect to rentals and tenant selec
tion: (a) it may rent or lease the dwelling accommo
dations therein only to persons or families of low
income who lack the amount of income which is neces
sary (as determined by the Municipal Housing Com
mission undertaking the housing project) to enable
them, without financial assistance, to live in decent,
safe and sanitary dwellings, without overcrowding,
and at rentals within the financial reach of such per
sons of low income ; (b) it may rent or lease to a
tenant dwelling accommodations consisting of the
number of rooms (but no greater number) which it
deems necessary to provide safe and sanitary ac
commodations to the proposed occupants thereof,
without overcrowding; and (c) it shall not accept
any person as a tenant in any housing project if the
person or persons who would occupy the dwelling
accommodations have an annual net income in excess
of five times the annual rental of the quarters to be
furnished by such person or persons, except that in
the case of families with three or more minor de
pendents, such ratio shall not exceed six to one; in
computing the rental for this purpose of selecting
tenants, there shall be included in the rental the
average annual cost (as determined by the Municipal
Housing Commission) to occupants of heat, water,
electricity, gas, cooking range and other necessary
services or facilities, whether or not the charge for
such services and facilities is in fact included in the
rental.

low rent housing projects undertaken by Municipal
Housing Commissions shall not be operated for
profit and shall be available only to persons of low
income, and at rentals within the financial reach of
persons of low income, an emergency is hereby de
clared to exist, and this Act shall become effective
immediately after its passage and approval by the
Governor. Approved by the Governor March 22, 1940.

CHAPTER 29 235
or owing to such dealers in such goods, wares or merchandise;
and to require said persons, firms or corporations so engaged
in financing such loans to furnish said dealers semi-annual
reports covering all transactions relating to the holdback and
reserve accounts of such dealers; and declaring when such
holdbacks or reserves shall become due and payable; pro
viding a penalty for the violation of this act.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
% 1. That all persons, firms or corporations en
gaged in the business of financing loans on property,
goods, wares or merchandise sold by dealers thereof
to purchasers on credit, shall pay interest at the rate
of two and one-half per cent (2M>%) per annum on
holdbacks or reserves or other monies withheld from
the dealers from whom such goods, wares or mer
chandise have been purchased, under the contracts
which said person, firm or corporation have made
with respect to financing such purchases on credit,
said interest to be paid such dealers semi-annually
on the first of January and the first of July of each
year.
§ 2. That such persons, firms or corporations en
gaged in making such loans shall furnish each dealer
as of January first and July first of each year, a
report showing the status of such dealers reserve or
holdback account.
§ 3. That any amount withheld by said persons,
firm or corporation engaged in making said loans
shall become due and payable immediately upon the
close of any loan account.
§ 4. Any person, firm or corporation engaged in
making loans as hereinabove described who shall fail
to comply with the provisions of this Act shall be
deemed guilty of a misdemeanor and shall upon con
viction be punished by a fine of not less than $10.00
nor more than $50.00 for each separate offense.
Neither approved nor vetoed.

CHAPTER 35 251
CHAPTER 35
(H. B. 64)
AN ACT adopting as the Civil and Criminal Codes of Practice
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, the supplement to Carroll's
Kentucky Codes, Civil and Criminal, compiled and edited by
William Edward Baldwin and Richard Priest Dietzman, issued
In the year 1939.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
That the supplement of Carroll's Kentucky
Codes of Practice, Civil and Criminal, compiled and
edited by William Edward Baldwin and Richard
Priest Dietzman, issued in the year 1939, be and
the same is hereby adopted as the Civil and Criminal
Codes of Practice of the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky, and in all Civil and Criminal proceedings,
the Civil and Criminal Codes contained therein,
may be cited or referred to as the Civil and Criminal
Codes of Practice of the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky by reference to, or citation of any of said
sections, and any of the sections therein may be
amended or repealed by the General Assembly of
the Commonwealth of Kentucky in the manner now
provided by reference to and citation of the section
of said supplement to Carroll's Codes of Practice
without giving the date or title of the act from
which the section is taken.
Whereas this act will save a great deal of labor
and time in the investigation and enactment of
laws, and will prevent confusion and uncertainty,
an emergency is declared to exist, and this act shall
take effect and be in force from and after its ap
proval by the Governor.
All acts or parts of acts in conflict with this act
are hereby repealed.
Approved March 18, 1940.

252 ACTS or THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
CHAPTER 36
(S. B. 123)
AN ACT relating to garnishment on judgments in personam.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
A new section of the Civil Code of Practice is
created, to be numbered section 194a and to read
as follows :
(1) Any person in whose favor a final judgment
in personam has been entered in any court of record
of this state may, upon filing affidavit in the office
of the clerk of the court in which the judgment was
entered, and in the same cause in which said judg
ment was obtained showing the date of the judg
ment and the amount due thereon, and that one or
more named persons hold property belonging to,
or are indebted to, the judgment debtor, obtain an
order of garnishment directed to the sheriff, with
as many copies as the plaintiff may direct, requiring
him to summon the garnishees named in the affidavit
to answer on the return day of the order, and to
make due return thereof. The order shall be made
returnable as an order of arrest is directed to be
returned.
(2) The judgment plaintiff shall not be required
to execute bond to obtain the order.
(3) The order of garnishment shall be served on
the persons named as garnishees in the manner pro
vided in subsection 3 of section 203 of the Civil Code
of Practice, and in addition a copy thereof shall be
served on the judgment debtor.
(4) The judgment debtor may, on the return
day, appear and claim the exemption of any property
or debt that is exempt from execution, and on proof
of exemption the garnishment shall be discharged as
to the exempt property or debt.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 299

classification.title: AN ACT relating to garnishment on judgments in personam.
classification.summary: This act creates a new section (194a) of the Civil Code of Practice, allowing a judgment creditor to obtain an order of garnishment against a third party holding property of or indebted to the judgment debtor. The judgment plaintiff is not required to post a bond. The order must be served on the garnishee and the judgment debtor, who can claim exemptions on the return day.

a revolving fund set up by the Department of
Finance in each of said years to be used by the
Secretary of State for supplies, books, postage,
equipment, personnel and necessary expenses to
enforce section 540 of the Kentucky Statutes, and
all expenditures from these revolving funds shall
be approved by the Commissioner of Finance as
provided by law.
§ 3. Whereas, domestic corporations are required
by the provisions of Section 4225, Kentucky Statutes,
Carroll's 1936 Edition, to pay into the State
Treasury a tax of one-tenth of one per cent upon
the amount of the capital stock which such corpora
tions are authorized to issue, and a like tax upon
any subsequent increase thereof, and
Whereas, no similar tax is required of foreign
corporations, the fee of twenty-five dollars herein
required of foreign corporations for filing their
articles of incorporation, and the fee for recording
said articles and amendments thereto against both
domestic and foreign corporations, are necessary
to enable the Secretary of State to perform the ad
ditional duties required of him by this Act, and suf
ficient funds have not been appropriated to enable
him to perform the duties required of his office
under the provisions of this Act, an emergency is
hereby declared to exist, and this Act shall take
effect immediately upon its adoption by the General
Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky and
its approval by the Governor.
Approved March 26, 1940

CHAPTER 43
_)-' (H. B. 334)
AN ACT to amend and re-enact section 883b-3 of the Kentucky
Statutes, Carroll's Edition 1936.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. That Section 883b-3 of the Kentucky
Statutes, Carroll's Edition 1936, be amended so that
when amended Section 883b-3 shall read as fol
lows :
"No such sale shall be valid unless consented to
by the holders of not less than three-fourths (f)
of the capital stock of the vendor corporation which
consent shall be given either in writing or by vote
at a special meeting of the said stockholders called
for that purpose upon the same notice as that re
quired for the annual meetings of the corporation,
which notice shall clearly state the purpose for
which the meeting is called. Any stockholder of the
vendor corporation may notify its secretary in
writing on or before the date of such meeting that
he objects to the proposed sale. If the proposed
sales shall be consummated, the vendee, therein,
thirty (30) days thereafter, upon the demand of
such dissenting stockholder and upon his surrender
of his stock therein to the vendor corporation for
cancellation, shall pay to such dissenting stockholder
the market value of his stock, which shall in no event
be less than the book value of said stock, according
to the last balance sheet of the selling corporation.
Any amount so paid by the vendee to dissenting
stockholders shall be deducted from the purchase
price of the property in question. Nothing in this
section shall apply to mortgages or deeds of trust
executed by corporations."
Neither approved nor vetoed.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 312

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact section 883b-3 of the Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's Edition 1936.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 883b-3 of the Kentucky Statutes to establish a process for the sale of a corporation's assets. It requires consent from three-fourths of the capital stock holders, either in writing or by vote at a special meeting. Dissenting stockholders have the right to demand the market value (not less than book value) of their stock from the vendee within thirty days of the sale's consummation, with this amount deductible from the purchase price. The section does not apply to mortgages or deeds of trust.

select an engineer. The engineer shall be approved
by the fiscal court and shall draw up the plans and
specifications giving a full description and the
details and manner of construction of the structures
to be constructed or improved. When the plans
and specifications have been approved by the
corporation and the fiscal court they shall be filed
in the office of the county court clerk.
§ 4. (1) The fiscal court may then enter into

thereon have been completed, for a term of one
year. The contract shall give the county the right
and option to extend the term of such lease from the
expiration of the original term for terms of one

WHEREAS, the change of time for holding terms of the Circuit
Courts in the Thirty-eighth Judicial District has become
necessary because of a conflict in time of holding said courts
and the District Court of the United States for the Western
District of Kentucky which convenes at Bowling Green and
wherein is pending a large volume of litigation affecting
citizens of said Thirty-eighth Judicial District and wherein
attorneys of said Thirty-eighth Judicial District are attorneys
for many of said litigants with cases in said United States
District Court and because of such conflict in time of holding
said courts.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. That Section 965-38 of the Supplement of
Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1939 Edition in so far
as it relates to the time of holding the terms of Cir
cuit Court in the Thirty-eighth Judicial District com
posed of the counties of Ohio, Muhlenberg, Butler
and Edmonson, be and the same is hereby amended
by striking from said section all that part which
relates to the time of holding the terms of Circuit
Courts in the Thirty-eighth Judicial District and
inserting in lieu thereof the following, so that as
amended it shall read as follows :
THIRTY-EIGHTH JUDICIAL DISTRICT
Ohio County, at Hartford ; first Monday in April,
twelve juridical days; first Monday in July, twelve
juridical days ; first Monday in October, twelve jurid
ical days; first Monday in December, twelve juridi
cal days.
Muhlenberg County, at Greenville; first Monday
in January, twenty-four juridical days; first Mon
day in May, twenty-four juridical days; first Mon
day in September, twenty-four juridical days.

Grand Juries be summoned and criminal and penal cases be

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. That Section 965-26 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes of 1936 Edition, relating to the 26th Circuit
Court District be, and the same is hereby amended
by striking out the whole of Section 965-26, to wit:
"Bell County—at Pineville and Middlesboro —on
the first Monday in January, twenty-four juridical
days ; fourth Monday in February, eighteen juridical
days ; second Monday in April, eighteen juridical
days; fourth Monday in May, twenty-four juridical
days; second Monday in September, eighteen jurid
ical days ; first Monday in November, eighteen
juridical days.
Harlan county — at Harlan —first Monday in Feb
ruary, eighteen juridical days; third Monday in

eighteen juridical days; third Monday in August,
eighteen juridical days; first Monday in October,
twenty-four juridical days; fourth Monday in No

Grand Juries shall be summoned and criminal and
penal cases shall be heard only at the terms of Court

day in November; unless in case of emergency the
Court may otherwise direct".

§ 1. That Section 1055 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes, Baldwin's 1936 Revision, is hereby repealed
and re-enacted so that when it is re-enacted it shall
provide as follows :
Section 1055. The Judge of the Quarterly Court
shall keep a record of all of his official acts out of
term time, and shall keep a docket, order book and
execution book, in which shall be recorded as Clerks
are required to do, the proceedings of the Court, and
shall fix a rule day for the return of executions and
other processes. The record books shall be paid for
by the County. He may appoint a Clerk of his Court
who shall have power to file papers and issue all proc
esses and administer the oaths, and to issue war
rants of arrest and criminal summons for offenses
within the jurisdiction of said Court, after there has
been filed before said Clerk the proper affidavit.
§ 2. Four new sections of the statutes are created
to be numbered and read as follows :
Section 1055a-l. In counties having a population
of 250,000 or more the County Judge elected at a
regular election for said office, shall, on the first Mon
day in July succeeding his election, appoint for a
period of four years a Clerk and five Deputy Clerks
of the Quarterly Court, who shall have the power to
file papers and issue all processes, and for their serv
ices the Clerk shall be paid at the rate of $3,000.00
per annum and each of the Deputy Clerks at the rate
of $1,500.00, per annum, said salaries to be paid in
monthly installments by the Fiscal Court of the
County in which said Clerk and his Deputies are ap
pointed, out of the County Treasury, from money
raised for County Purposes, and fees collected from
the Quarterly Court shall be paid into the Treasury
of the County. Said collections shall be made by
the Clerk, who shall monthly settle with the County

Fees collected from the Quarterly Court are to be paid into the County Treasury.

The act also addresses the timing of court sessions in the 36th Judicial District (Breathitt, Wolfe, and Magoffin counties).

CHAPTER 59

AN ACT making it unlawful for any person to shoot into, or
throw any deadly weapon into, any dwelling house, or other
buildings wherein people reside or stay, and fixing penalties
for violation thereof.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
If any person shall willfully and maliciously
shoot into, or throw any kind of a deadly weapon
into, any dwelling house, or store house, or any
building where people live or frequent for pleasure
or business, shall be guilty of a felony and upon con
viction shall be confined in the penitentiary for not
less than two, nor more than twenty-one years.
All laws or parts of laws in conflict herewith, are
hereby repealed.
Approved by Governor, 22nd of March 1940.
CHAPTER 60
(S. B. 238)

any sort of snakes or reptiles in connection with any religious
service or gathering, and fixing penalties.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. No person shall display, handle or use any
kind of snake or reptile in connection with any re
ligious service or gathering.
(a) Any person violating the provisions of this
Act shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and punished
by a fine of not less than $50.00 nor more than
$100.00.
Neither approved nor disapproved.

AN ACT to provide for the purchase of liability and indemnity
insurance against negligence of school bus drivers, and author
izing school boards to purchase same.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Each board of education shall have power
and authority to set aside funds to provide for
liability and indemnity insurance against the
negligence of the drivers or operators of school
busses owned or operated by the board. The in
demnity bond or insurance policy shall be issued
by some surety or insurance company or other in
surance carrier duly authorized to transact business
as such within this Commonwealth, and shall bind
the surety, insurance company, or insurance carrier
to pay any final judgment or judgments rendered
against the insured or policy holder arising out of
the death or injury to any school child or school
children, for loss or damage to property of any such
school children, or death or injury to any person
or persons.

CHAPTER 67 299
"WHEREAS, the Preamble of the Constitution of Kentucky,
states in part, that, we, as a people are, 'grateful to Almighty
God for civil, political, and religious liberties — '; and
"WHEREAS, these civil, political and religous liberties are best
secured and furthered by providing moral instruction for
pupils in connection with the Public Schools of the State;
therefore,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
"§ 1. Boards of Education may provide for
moral instruction. — The Boards of Education of in
dependent and county school districts are hereby
authorized and empowered to provide for the moral
instruction of pupils subject to the jurisdiction of
such boards of education in the manner herein
after provided in this act.
"§ 2. Survey of religious preferences may be
made. —The board of education of each school dis
trict may authorize a complete survey of all the
pupils attending the public schools within said
district and ascertain therefrom those pupils who
desire this instruction and have the consent of
parent or guardian for said instruction as provided
in this act.
"§ 3. Time and place of instruction to be de
termined by boards of education. — On a day in each
week, to be fixed by said boards of education, such
pupils, as express desire for moral instruction may
be excused for at least one hour, for the purpose
of attending their respective places of worship, or
some other suitable place, there to receive moral
instruction in accordance with the religious faith or
preference of the said pupils.
"§ 4. Rules and regulations. —Each school board
may make such arrangements with the respective
persons in charge of the moral instruction as the
said board deems necessary and advisable.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
That no person or persons connected with the
public school system of Kentucky shall use or cause
to be used, read or taught in the public schools of
Kentucky any text book or printed matter of any
kind whatsoever, which contains any statement or
statements which criticizes, condemns or contains
derogatory statements which could be deemed dis
crediting, discriminating, or harmful to any legally
constituted school or system of healing, science, or
profession in the state of Kentucky.
Penalty:—Any one violating the provisions of this
statute shall be subject to a fine of from fifty dol
lars ($50.00) to five hundred dollars ($500.00) or
imprisonment in the County jail for, from thirty
days to six months or both.
Neither approved nor vetoed.

(2) Section 4399-25, Kentucky Statutes. Election
by Secret Ballot; Nominating Petitions; Ballots;
General Election Laws Applicable; Procedure. —
All elections for members of boards of education
shall be by secret ballot. The ballot shall be on a
separate sheet from all other ballots used in any
election. The county clerk shall cause to be printed
on the ballot the names of legally eligible candidates
in whose behalf he is petitioned to do so in writing,
by not less than fifty legal voters of the county school
district division or independent school district as
the case may be; provided that in independent
school districts embracing cities of the first and
second class the number of such petitioners shall
not be less than one hundred. The petitions must
be filed in the office of the county clerk not more
than sixty and not less than fifteen days before
the day of election, and each petition must be signed
by the requisite number of qualified persons, and
shall show the district or the division of the county
district, as the case may be, and the place of resi
dence of each person signing it. In county school
district divisions no person shall sign more than
one petition, and in independent school districts
not more petitions than the number of offices to be
filled.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. In every county school district where all of
the subdistricts thereof levy a special tax, the board
of education of the county district may, in its
option, abolish all of the special levies of the sub-

board of education may levy a general tax of not
more than the maximum levy permitted for county
school districts increased by not more than the low
est of the subdistrict levies.
Inasmuch as school budgets must be made on or
before March 20, an emergency is declared to exist,
and this act shall become effective immediately upon
its passage and approval by the Governor.
Neither approved nor vetoed.

classification.title: An Act relating to officers of election for primary elections.
classification.summary: This act repeals and re-enacts Section 1550-22 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 Baldwin's Revised Edition. It outlines the appointment of election officers for primary elections by the county board of election commissioners. It specifies that party committees must submit lists of names at least twenty days before the primary, and these lists are open for inspection. Bona fide candidates, regardless of party, can jointly designate election officers through written notice to the board at least fifteen days before the primary. A group of twenty-five percent of candidates can have one person appointed in each precinct for which a name is submitted. Designated persons are to be appointed in equal proportion to clerk, sheriff, and judge offices across precincts. Fifty percent of candidates are entitled to designate further.

It defines terms related to abandonment and death, outlines how property is reduced to cash and transferred to the Treasurer, and details the process for rightful claimants to recover the property. It also addresses liability relief for those transferring property, provides for enforcement, and includes provisions for fines, penalties, and imprisonment for non-compliance. The act repeals several previous statutes related to escheatment.
classification.reasoning: The statute's title and text clearly indicate its purpose is to govern the escheatment of property. There is no mention of race, segregation, or any other element typically associated with Jim Crow laws. The act deals with general property law and the state's right to claim abandoned or unclaimed assets. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 1(506) of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edition, relating to escheat property.
classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes concerning property within Kentucky that is not claimed by heirs or devisees within eight years of the owner's death, or property that has been abandoned. Such property will vest in the Commonwealth, be liquidated, and the proceeds, after costs, will be paid to the Department of Revenue. This applies to all property except a perfect title to a corporeal hereditament.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the disposition of unclaimed or abandoned property within the Commonwealth of Kentucky. It outlines a process for the state to take possession of such assets, liquidate them, and deposit the proceeds into the Department of Revenue. There is no mention of race, and the provisions apply universally to any unclaimed or abandoned property, regardless of the owner's race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to escheats and abandoned property
classification.summary: This act amends existing statutes concerning the escheat of property to the state. It establishes a presumption of death without heirs for individuals not known to be living for seven successive years, leading to the liquidation of their real and personal estate and payment of proceeds to the Department of Revenue. It also defines conditions under which bank deposits payable on demand are presumed abandoned after ten years of inactivity and are to be reported to the Department of Revenue.

ment of Revenue at public sale at Frankfort, or in

the sale shall be held at the courthouse door.
