§ 4. For his services in selling the special
licenses and collecting and remitting to the Commis
sion the said voluntary contributions to said fund,
the county clerk shall be entitled to a commission of
10% of all such voluntary contributions so collected
by him, in addition to the compensation now allowed
him for issuing licenses as now provided by law, and
said commission shall be deducted by him when re
mitting his collections to the Commission. The Com
mission is further authorized at its discretion to set
aside a sum not exceeding One Thousand ($1,000)
in any year, to buy appropriate prizes for the four
clerks in the state who collect the largest amounts in
such voluntary contributions in proportion to the
amounts of money received from the regular price
of such licenses sold in their respective counties.
The Commission shall make such rules and regula
tions as it deems proper for awarding such prizes.
§ 5. Any person who makes a donation of Ten
Dollars ($10) for a hunting license, or Ten Dollars
($10) for fishing license in each of five successive
years, or five of the same kind and grade of licenses
in one year, shall be entitled to a hunting license, or
a fishing license, as the case may be, good during his
life-time, and the same shall be issued to him upon
his application to the Commission. In like manner
a non-resident of Kentucky may obtain a life-time
license for either hunting or fishing by buying ten
successive licenses at a price equal to the regular
price of the non-resident's license plus Ten ($10)
Dollars, for each regular license so purchased.

CHAPTER 87 359
§ 6. All funds coming into the hands of the Com
mission for the use of said "Kentucky Sportsmen's
Voluntary Fund" shall be earmarked and used for
no other purpose whatsoever. After paying the
necessary fees and expenses for the collection and
administration of such fund said fund shall be
expended as follows : So much of said fund as comes
from the sale of special hunting licenses shall be
used solely for the purchase of game for distri
bution. So much of said fund as come from the sale
of special fishing licenses shall be used solely to pur
chase and raise fish to be released in Kentucky
waters that the Commission deems best.
§ 7. The Commission shall have power and
authority to make and enforce all rules and regula
tions it deems necessary to carry out the provisions
of this Act.
§ 8. All acts or parts of acts which are in conflict
or inconsistent with thia act are, to the extent of
such conflict or inconsistency, hereby repealed.
§ 9. If any part of this act, or any order or rule
of the Commission made or issued in pursuance of
this act, shall for any reason be adjudged, by any
court of competent jurisdiction, to be unconstitu
tional or invalid, such judgment shall not affect,
impair or invalidate the remainder thereof, but shall
be confined in its operation to such part of this act
or such rule or order directly involved in contro
versy in which said judgment shall have been
rendered. Approved by the Governor March 15, 1940.

tent jurisdiction shall be fined not less than fifty
dollars nor more than five hundred or be subject to
imprisonment for not less than ten days nor more
than sixty days or both fine and imprisonment.

sunset.

CHAPTER 93
(S. B. 53)
AN ACT to amend section 429 of the Criminal Code of Practice,
and to provide for appeals from judgments upon trials under
writs of habeas corpus.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Section 429 of the Criminal Code of
Practice is amended to read as follows:
429. The judgment upon a trial under a writ of
habeas corpus shall not prevent the issuing of
subsequent writs for the same cause, nor be given
in evidence on the trial under a subsequent writ,
but if an appeal was taken from the previous
judgment that fact may be given in evidence and
shall bar a trial under the subsequent writ. No
officer, except a circuit judge, who has tried a writ
shall try a subsequent writ issued for the same
cause.
§ 2. Two sections are added to the Criminal
Code of Practice to be numbered and read as follows :
429-1. Appeal. —Any party to a trial under a
writ of habeas corpus may appeal to the Court of
Appeals by filing with the clerk of the Court of
Appeals, within ten days after the entry of the
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 422

classification.title: AN ACT to amend section 429 of the Criminal Code of Practice, and to provide for appeals from judgments upon trials under writs of habeas corpus.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 429 of the Criminal Code of Practice regarding habeas corpus trials. It clarifies that judgments on habeas corpus writs do not prevent subsequent writs for the same cause, nor can they be used as evidence in subsequent trials, unless an appeal was taken from the previous judgment, in which case it bars a subsequent trial. It also restricts officers, other than circuit judges, from trying subsequent writs for the same cause. Additionally, it adds provisions for appeals to the Court of Appeals from judgments on habeas corpus trials.

child shall not survive the adopted child, then in
that event the property of the deceased adopted
child without lawful issue shall go to the natural
parents in the line of descent and under the same
provisions as are now or may hereafter be provided
by law for descent and distribution; but in the
event that the adopted child without lawful issue
shall not be survived by either of its adopted or
natural parents, then his property shall descend to
his natural and adopted relatives, share and share
alike, according to Section 1393 of Carroll's Ken
tucky Statutes, 1936 Edition. Such child shall be
freed from all legal obligations or maintenance and
obedience to such natural parents; provided, that
where the adoptive parent of such child shall be
married to one of the natural parents of such child,
then the relation of such child towards such parent
shall in no way be altered by such adoption, and
the natural rights and obligations of such natural
and adoptive parents towards such child shall be
the same as if such child were the natural child
of both the natural parent and the adoptive parent.
Nothing herein shall be so construed as to prevent
the adopted child from inheriting under the general
law in regard to the descent and distribution from
its natural parents.
§ 7. A subsequent adoption is authorized by this
act and in such a case the words "father, mother or
parent" include father, mother, or parent by
adoption.

CHAPTER 97
(H. B. 99)
AN ACT providing that any husband, wife, heir-at-law, bene
ficiary under a will, or beneficiary under an insurance policy
forfeits right to inherit or receive property from person he or
she kills and is convicted therefor.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
That if the husband, wife, heir-at-law, beneficiary
under a will, joint tenant with the right of sur
vivorship or the beneficiary under any insurance
policy shall have taken the life of the decedent and
be convicted therefor of a crime which is a felony,
the person so convicted forfeits all interest in and to
the property of the decedent, including any in
terest he would receive as surviving joint tenant, and
the property interest so forfeited descends to the
decedent's other heirs-at-law, unless otherwise
disposed of by the decedent.
Approved March 18, 1940.

classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for the approval and operation of hospital service corporations in Kentucky. It mandates that the Commissioner of Insurance and the State Board of Health must approve the formation of such corporations based on public need, ability to perform services, and public convenience and necessity. It also requires the State Board of Health to approve all contracts for hospital service and sets regulations for these contracts, including maximum contract holders based on hospital bed capacity. The act also states that the rates charged by these corporations are subject to the approval of the Commissioner of Insurance and that these corporations are intended to be non-profit.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details regulations for hospital service corporations, focusing on approval processes, contract forms, and rate approvals by state officials. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The regulations appear to be administrative and economic in nature, aimed at overseeing the provision of hospital services. Therefore, it is classified as not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act for the appointment of a Custodian of Insurance Securities
classification.summary: This act requires domestic life insurance companies to deposit securities for the security and benefit of their policyholders. It outlines the appointment of a Custodian of Insurance Securities, the payment of their salary and expenses, and the funding of these provisions by the affected companies. A threshold of twenty million dollars in legal reserve or policy valuation is set, beyond which further deposits are not required unless the company elects to represent that its reserves are on deposit.

classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for domestic life insurance companies to deposit securities with the state. § 12. Section 762-16 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes, 1936 edition, is amended to read as
follows :
762-16. Insurance Companies to Deposit Secu
rities with Custodian of Insurance Securities.—
Every insurance company doing business in the State
of Kentucky, and now required by law or herein
after required by law to make deposits of stock,
bonds, notes, real estate, mortgages or other
evidences of indebtedness or ownership of property
for the protection and benefit of its policyholders,
shall make such deposits with the Custodian of
Insurance Securities, who shall hold them in his
official capacity for the security and benefit of the
policyholders of the company making such deposits
and also as trustee for the insurance company.

classification.title: An Act relating to the licensing of non-resident insurance agents.
classification.summary: This statute outlines the requirements for non-resident insurance agents to obtain a license to do business in Kentucky. It details the application process, including required information about the applicant's age, residence, occupation, business history, familiarity with Kentucky insurance laws, intent to conduct business in good faith, and any past license refusals, revocations, or suspensions in other states. It also requires disclosure of any financial interests or control over Kentucky-licensed insurance agencies, agents, or solicitors. § 7. Penalties. —Any contractor or subcontrac
tor who shall violate either the wage or work hours
provision provided in contracts under the provisions
of this Act, or shall suffer, permit or require any

fixed, shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and upon

offense, and the violation as concerns each individual
laborer or worker shall constitute a separate offense.
Any public authority who willfully fails or refuses
either to comply or to require compliance with any
of the provisions of this Act, shall be guilty of a
misdemeanor and upon conviction shall be fined not
more than $100.00 for each offense, in the discretion
of the jury.
§ 8. Severability Clause. —If any paragraph,
section, sentence, clause or provision of this Act
shall be held invalid or inapplicable, such holding
shall not invalidate the remaining provisions or por
tions of this Act or the application thereof to any
other person, situation or circumstances to which
the remaining portions may be applicable; and it
hereby is declared to be the legislative intent that
this Act shall be liberally construed to effectuate the
purposes herein expressed, and the remaining por
tions of said Act would have been enacted without
the inclusion of such invalid or inapplicable portion
thereof.

§ 1. Definitions.—When used in this Act, unless

ing definitions shall apply:

§ 1. There is hereby created in the Department
of Industrial Relations the Kentucky Industrial
Safety Board consisting of five members, two mem
bers representing industry and two members
representing labor. Such members shall be ap
pointed by the Governor from a list of prospective
members recommended by bona fide associations
representative of industry and labor. Said members
shall be appointed for a period of three years and
shall receive their actual and necessary expenses
incurred in carrying out the duties herein provided.
The Commissioner of Industrial Relations shall be
ex-officio a member of said Board and shall act
as its chairman.
§ 2. Definitions. —When used in this article :
(a) The terms "safe" and "safety" shall mean
such freedom from danger to the life and safety
of employees as the nature and place of employment
reasonably permits.
(b) "Safety devices" and "safeguards" shall
be given a broad interpretation so as to include any
reasonable and practicable device which the nature
of the employment reasonably permits in protecting
the life or safety of the employee.

§ 6. In re Hearings and Judicial Reviews.
(a) Appeals from general rules. Any person
claiming to be aggrieved by any general rules,
orders, or regulations of the Kentucky Industrial
Safety Board, shall have the right to appeal to the
Franklin Circuit Court for review of such general
rules, orders or regulations.
(b) Any person claiming to be aggrieved by any
ruling of the Kentucky Industrial Safety Board,
upon any specific complaint, shall have the right to
appeal to the Circuit Court of the county in which
the place of employment complained of is located.
Such appeal shall be filed within sixty days after
the service of any final order and the appellant
thereof shall petition such court for rehearing
upon any of the following grounds and upon no
other :
1. That the Kentucky Industrial Safety Board
acted without or in excess of its powers,
2. That the order or decision was procured by
fraud or mistake,
3. That the order, decision, rule or regulation is
unreasonable.
The Circuit Court after hearing the parties shall
enter judgment affirming, modifying or reversing
the order of the Board or in advance of judgment
may remand the matter to the Board for further
proceedings as directed by the Court.
(c) An appeal from the decision of the Circuit
Court after hearing as provided in paragraphs
('a) and (b) of this section, shall be granted to the
Court of Appeals.

termining the reason or reasons for such labor

under oath; any findings of fact made by him in

Nothing in this section shall be construed as giving

In the event of a labor dispute between an em
ployer and his employees, or their authorized repre
sentatives, which the parties are unable to settle
between themselves and the services of the Com
missioner as conciliator and mediator have been
accepted by both parties to the labor dispute, no
strike or lockout shall take place or be put into effect
pending the efforts of the Commissioner or his
duly authorized representatives to conciliate and
mediate such dispute. Upon acceptance of the
services of the Commissioner as conciliator and
mediator by both parties to a labor dispute, it shall
be his duty to promptly investigate and undertake to
conciliate and mediate such labor dispute without
delay; no agreement accepting the service of the
Commissioner shall be binding on either party to
such an agreement for a longer term than fifteen
days, except that such term of days may be extended
by mutual consent of the parties to the agreement.
Nothing in this Section shall apply where a joint
wage agreement now exists or may hereafter exist
in any industry which by its terms and provisions
provides for settlement of disputes. Any dispute
which may arise shall be settled by the terms and
provisions of said contract. Nothing contained in
Article III of this Act shall apply where the
authority of a federal agency has been invoked or
a federal agency assumes jurisdiction. In the event
the Commissioner has assumed jurisdiction and a
federal agency thereafter assumes jurisdiction, the
authority of the Commissioner shall cease pending
federal jurisdiction.

at wholesale or retail.

No license shall confer authority upon more than

business as a peddler either for himself or as the
agent of another, shall procure a separate license.

into the State Treasury through the Department
of Revenue and credited to the General Expenditure

442 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
§ 9. Penalties.
Any peddler, as defined herein, who shall peddle
or sell or offer or expose for sale any goods, wares
or merchandise without at the time having procured
and having then and there in his possession the
license herein required, shall be guilty of a mis
demeanor and upon conviction therefor shall be
punished by a fine of not less than $50.00 nor more
than $100.00 for each offense. Each day such busi
ness is carried on in violation of this Act shall
constitute a separate offense.
§ 10. Enacted Under Police Powers.
It is declared to be the legislative intent that this
Act is enacted under the police powers of the Com
monwealth and in the interests of the public welfare.
§ 11. Administration.
The administration of this Act shall be under the
general supervision of the Department of Revenue
which shall prepare and furnish all necessary
forms.
§ 12. Clerks Fees.
The Clerk, for filing application and issuing
license under this Act, shall receive a fee of $1.00
for each license.
§ 13. Provisions Severable.
The provisions of this Act are severable. If any
provision, section, or paragraph of this Act, or the
application thereof to any person, shall be held
to be invalid, such decision shall not effect or impair
the remainder of the Act, it being hereby declared
the legislative intent to license and tax separately
each person contemplated by the provisions of this
Act.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. That section 1, Chapter 110, Acts of the
1938 General Assembly, be repealed, amended and
re-enacted so that when amended and re-enacted it
shall read as follows :
The following words and phrases shall for the
purposes of the Operator's License Law have the
meanings respectively ascribed to them in this sec
tion, except in those instances where the context
clearly indicates a different meaning.
(a) "Vehicle." —Every device in, upon or by
which any person or property is or may be trans
ported or drawn upon a public highway or street,
excepting devices moved by human and animal power
or used exclusively upon stationary rails or tracks.
(b) "Motor Vehicle." —Every vehicle, as herein
defined, which is self-propelled.

classification.summary: This act amends a statute concerning non-resident drivers. It allows non-residents over 16 who are licensed in their home state to drive in Kentucky, provided their home state offers similar privileges to Kentucky residents. It also permits non-residents whose home states do not require licensing to drive for up to 30 days annually if their vehicle is registered, though they may be asked to prove ownership and identity. The Department retains the right to refuse or discontinue these exemptions.

§ 4. That section 3, Chapter 110, Acts of the

(a) An operator's license shall not be granted to

been suspended at any time during the period for

whose operator's license has been revoked under
the provisions of the Operator's License Law, nor
to any non-resident whose privilege of exemption
under section 3 hereof has been refused or dis
continued, until the expiration of one year after such

to any person who is an habitual drunkard or is
addicted to the use of narcotic drugs.

It also mandates the forwarding of operator license certificates for offenses with mandatory revocation to the Department. A penalty of $100 is imposed for failure to report.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative reporting of motor vehicle operator convictions and license revocations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly segregate or discriminate based on race. The focus is purely on traffic law enforcement and administrative procedures. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.summary: This statute outlines offenses related to motor vehicle operation that would lead to the denial or revocation of a driver's license. These offenses include manslaughter, assault and battery, driving under the influence, perjury related to motor vehicle laws, felonies committed using a motor vehicle, multiple reckless driving charges, and failing to stop after an accident. It also addresses the extension of license denial periods if a person operates a vehicle while their license is already denied, suspended, or revoked.

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 7, Chapter 110, Acts of the 1938 General Assembly, relating to the denial, suspension, or revocation of motor vehicle licenses.
classification.summary: This act amends existing law to grant the Department of Motor Vehicles or its agents the authority to deny, suspend, or revoke a person's motor vehicle license or privilege to operate a vehicle in Kentucky. This can be done with or without a hearing or conviction record if the Department has reason to believe the person has committed offenses for which mandatory revocation is provided, caused or contributed to an accident resulting in death, injury, or serious property damage, is incompetent or afflicted with infirmities rendering driving unsafe, is a habitually reckless or negligent driver, or obtained a license improperly.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative process of issuing, suspending, and revoking motor vehicle licenses based on driving behavior, competence, and adherence to traffic laws. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. The grounds for license action are based on objective criteria related to driving safety and legal compliance. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact Sections 2739m-53 and 2739m-59 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, Baldwin's 1936 Revision, relating to motor vehicle operator's licenses.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky statutes concerning motor vehicle operator's licenses. It clarifies that negligence of a minor under 18 driving a motor vehicle is imputed to the person who signed their license application, making them jointly and severally liable for damages. It also establishes that driving a motor vehicle with a denied, canceled, suspended, or revoked operator's license or withdrawn privilege is a misdemeanor.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly target or discriminate against any racial group. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact Section 9 of Chapter 110, Acts of the Regular Session of the 1938 General Assembly, relating to operator's licenses.
classification.summary: This act amends existing legislation regarding operator's licenses. It clarifies that drivers involved in accidents resulting in damage, who are ineligible for, have failed to procure, or whose license is cancelled, suspended, or revoked, are deemed prima facie negligent. It also outlines the process for examining applicants for operator's licenses, including tests for eyesight, understanding of highway signs, knowledge of traffic laws, and practical driving ability. Examinations are to be held in the applicant's county of residence unless otherwise permitted. Persons holding a license prior to September 1, 1938, are exempt from this examination.

classification.title: An Act to amend and re-enact Sections 10 and 12 of Chapter 110 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1938, relating to operator's licenses.
classification.summary: This act amends and re-enacts sections of the 1938 Acts concerning operator's licenses. It clarifies the process for license reinstatement after denial, suspension, or revocation, requiring applicants to retake an examination. It also mandates that licensees notify the Department of Transportation in writing within ten days of any change in address or name, providing their old and new information and license number. Finally, it directs the Department to ensure an adequate supply of forms for administering the Operator's License Law, with the style and method of use to be prescribed by the Department to protect the Commonwealth's financial and safety interests.

It also allows for the revocation of ordinances related to these public spaces, changes in land use, and provides for their cost, maintenance, and regulation. The act declares an emergency.

472 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
' ' Section 3290-33. —To acquire, establish and main
tain public cemeteries, parks, squares, avenues,
promenades and fountains, either within or without
the city; and authorizing said cities of the third
class, by the enactment of appropriate ordinances,
to repeal ordinances that heretofore have been or
hereafter may be enacted creating such public ceme
teries, parks, squares, avenues, promenades and
fountains where the same were not acquired or
given to the city for such specific purposes, and au
thorizing said cities to provide, by appropriate ordi
nances, for the use of said lands, easements, build
ings and appurtenances thereon or appertaining
thereto for other purposes ; to make all necessary
appropriations for the cost and maintenance of
same, and to make regulations for the use, man
agement and direction thereof."
§ 2. That, whereas, many cities of the third class
have by ordinances established public cemeteries,
parks, squares, avenues, promenades and fountains
and have subsequently determined that they were
financially unable to properly improve, operate and
maintain the same; and,
Whereas, the growth and expansion of many
cities of the third class necessitate a change of use
of the land to purposes other than those provided
in former ordinances that have heretofore been
enacted, and because the public good demands that
such power and authority be vested in cities of the
third class to remedy such conditions as quickly
as possible, an emergency is hereby declared to exist
and this Act shall take immediate effect when passed
and approved by the Governor.
Approved by the Governor February 21, 1940.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 519

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 3290-33 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, relating to public cemeteries, parks, squares, avenues, promenades and fountains in cities of the third class.
classification.summary: This act grants cities of the third class the authority to acquire, establish, and maintain public spaces such as cemeteries, parks, and avenues. It also allows these cities to repeal previous ordinances that created such spaces if they were not acquired for specific purposes, and to repurpose the land for other uses. The act states that many cities are financially unable to maintain these spaces and that a change of use is necessary for the public good, declaring an emergency for immediate effect.

The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly administrative and not related to racial segregation or discrimination.

and all applicants must be twenty-one years of age
or over and must be able to read and write and

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. That Section 2925 of Chapter 19 of the Acts
of the General ASsembly of 1902, which is known as
Section 2925, Kentucky Statutes, Carroll's Edition,
1936, be amended, repealed and reenacted so as
when amended and reenacted the same shall read
as follows:
The Mayor of a city of the first class, is hereby
authorized to appoint a Judge Pro Tem, of the
Police Court who shall hold office at the pleasure
of the Mayor, and shall have the same rights, powers
and duties as the regular judge has, when from any
cause or reason, the regular judge of said Court
shall fail to attend and to hold court, or for any
cause or reason vacates the bench. Said Judge Pro.
Tem, shall be prohibited from practicing any case
in said Court.
The Judge Pro Tem, must possess the same
qualifications of the regular judge, and shall receive
a salary of $3,000.00 per annum, payable by the city,
semi-monthly.
Whereas, in every city of the first class the Judge
of the Police Court thereof is required to try each
day hundreds of cases; and
Whereas, the present judges are unable, because
of the pressure of business, to properly transact it ;
and

CHAPTER 117
(S. B. 109)
V AN ACT to amend Sections 3382 and 3383 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes, 193ti Edition.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Section 3382 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes,
1936 Edition, is amended to read as follows:
Between January 1st and February 1st of each
year the mayor shall, subject to the approval of
the council, appoint three intelligent housekeepers
residing in different wards of the city, who shall
constitute the board of tax supervisors for the
city.
§ 2. Section 3383 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes,
1936 Edition, is amended to read as follows :
The supervisors shall convene in the council hall
on the first Monday in February or at a later date,
but in no case later than the first Monday in April,
and proceed to the discharge of their duties. The
assessor shall meet with them and act as their
clerk. The supervisors shall supervise all assess
ments, and see that all property is assessed at its
fair cash value, taking into consideration the width,
depth and location of lots, and the cost and con
dition of improvements and personal property.
Neither approved nor disapproved.

§ 11. It is the deliberate intention of the General
ASsembly to enact each section of this bill separate
and if any section thereof shall be held invalid or
unconstitutional, the decision of the courts shall
not affect or impair any of the remaining sections
contained herein.
Whereas, a controversy has arisen concerning
the power of appointment of many of the employees
of the cities of the second class operating under the
Councilmanic form of government, and
Whereas, in at least one of the cities of the second
class operating under the Councilmatic form of
government, the Mayor and Board of Aldermen are
unable to agree upon appointments and by reason
of these facts, necessary governmental functions of
said city have been retarded and are now being re
tarded, and the welfare of the public is thus affected.
An Emergency is hereby declared to exist and this
act shall become effective immediately upon its
passage and approval by the Governor.
Approved by Governor Keen Johnson January 26, 1940.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the

Section 3235dd-33 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes,
Baldwin's 1936 Revision, is hereby amended and re-
enacted so that as amended and re-enacted it shall
read as follows:
The city manager shall be chosen by the board of
commissioners solely on the basis of his executive
and administrative qualifications. The choice shall
not be limited to inhabitants of the city or state. He
shall be employed for an indefinite period. He shall
be removable at any time without cause by the vote

sioners, and shall be removable for cause, including
willful neglect of duty, at any time by a majority

in the compensation of the city manager. If re

bers of the board of commissioners at any time after
having served for six months the city manager may

against him by the board and a public hearing on the
same before the board of commissioners prior to the
date on which his final removal shall take effect, but
during such hearing the board of commissioners
may suspend him from office. Such public hearing
shall be fixed by the board on a date not to exceed
thirty days after such demand therefor. The city

the city manager the board shall designate some

CHAPTER 123

AN ACT providing a notice to cities of the State of Kentucky of
intention to claim damages for any injuries.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. That no action shall be maintained against
any city in the State of Kentucky on account of any
injury growing out of any defect in the condition of
any bridge, boulevard, street, sidewalk, alley or
other public thoroughfare in the said cities unless
notice shall have been given the Mayor or City
Clerk, or Clerk of the Board of Aldermen of said
cities in the same manner as is provided for the
service of notice in actions in the Civil Code of
Practice. This notice shall be filed within ninety
'('90) days of the occurrence for which damage is
claimed, stating the time of and place where the
injury was received and the character and circum
stances of the injury, and that the person injured
will claim damages therefor from said city.
§ 2. All acts or parts of acts in conflict herewith
are hereby repealed to the extent of such conflict.
Approved by the Governor March 22, 1940.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. The Board of Trustees of a municipal
university in a city of the first class shall have the
power to acquire lands and improvements for the
purpose of expanding the plant and extending the
usefulness of such university by purchase or by gift,
and when unable to agree with the owner or owners
of such land and improvements necessary for the
purposes of such university, may proceed to condemn
and acquire such land and improvements, or so much
thereof as may be necessary for the purposes of such
university. All condemnation proceedings herein
provided for shall be conducted in the same manner
as provided by law for condemning land for park
purposes by the Board of Park Commissioners of
cities of the first class. Approved March 22, 1940.

It grants these boards broad powers over park management, including property acquisition, improvement, and the adoption of rules and regulations for park use.

classification.title: AN ACT conferring upon cities or municipal corporations in the Commonwealth of Kentucky the right and power, in the exercise of a governmental function, to use and employ their machinery and apparatus for the extinguishment of fires at any point in the same County in which such city or municipal corporation may be located or within a smaller territory with in such County, as may be fixed by the legislative body of such city; providing for such terms and regulations for such use of said apparatus as said cities may prescribe; and .releasing such cities, their officers, agents, servants and employees from any liability arising from the use and employment of such apparatus and machinery as herein provided, and providing that municipal firemen or firemen of fire prevention districts, full paid or volunteer, serving outside the corporate limits of a municipality, or fire protection district, in accordance with the provisions of this Act, shall be considered as serving in the regular line of duty.
classification.summary: This act grants cities and municipal corporations in Kentucky the authority to use their firefighting machinery and apparatus within their county or a designated smaller territory within that county. It also outlines terms and regulations for such use, releases cities and their personnel from liability arising from this use, and deems municipal or district firemen serving outside corporate limits under this act as serving in the regular line of duty.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the expansion of firefighting services by municipal corporations within their county. It does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by the chapter numbering and the introductory phrase 'AN ACT'.

516 ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
WHEREAS, In many Counties In the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky, there is a large amount of valuable property situated
outside of the corporate limits of various cities and towns
which could be saved from destruction by fire by the use and
employment of apparatus and machinery owned or controlled
by such towns kept and employed for the extinguishment of
fires; and WHEREAS, under present laws, such cities or
municipalities are prohibited from the use of such apparatus
for extinguishment of any fire outside of their corporate
limits, now therefore,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
Any municipality or city or town in the Common
wealth of Kentucky owning or having under its con
trol any apparatus or machinery designed for the
extinguishment of fires, may operate and conduct
such apparatus or machinery into any point in any
County in the Commonwealth of Kentucky in which
such city or municipality may be, located, or into a
smaller territory in such County as may be deter
mined by the legislative body of such city, or into
areas of another county or another state and may use
and employ such apparatus or machinery in the ex
tinguishment of any fire within such defined ter
ritory; and provided further, that such apparatus or
machinery shall be so used and conducted only in
conformity to such reasonable terms and regulations
which any such city or municipality may prescribe ;
and further, neither such city nor its officers, agents,
servants or employees shall be liable in any manner
whatever on account of such use or employment of
such machinery or apparatus at any point in any
such County outside of the corporate limit of such
city or municipality; and such use of such machinery
or apparatus shall be deemed to be employment and
use thereof in the exercise of a governmental function
of such city or municipality.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 563

classification.title: An Act relating to the operation of fire apparatus outside of municipal limits.
classification.summary: This act permits cities and municipalities in Kentucky to operate their fire extinguishing apparatus and machinery outside of their corporate limits, into any point in any county where the city is located, or into smaller defined territories within that county, or even into another county or state. It specifies that such use must conform to reasonable terms and regulations set by the city, and that neither the city nor its employees will be liable for damages incurred during such operations. The use of the apparatus outside the corporate limits is deemed a governmental function.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the operational scope of municipal fire departments, allowing them to respond to fires outside their immediate city limits. The text does not contain any explicit or implicit references to race, segregation, or differential treatment based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as indicated by 'ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY' and the year 1940. The confidence is high because the statute's content is clearly focused on municipal services and has no discernible racial component.

All municipal or fire protection district firemen,
full paid or volunteer, attending and serving at fire
or doing fire prevention work outside the corporate
limits of a municipality or the limits of fire protec
tion districts as hereinabove provided shall be con
sidered as serving in their regular line of duty as

limits of their own municipality or fire protection
district, but full paid men shall receive no additional
compensation and volunteer firemen shall receive
only such compensation as may be provided for by
ordinance or resolution for such cases, and all such
firemen shall be entitled to all of the benefits of any
pension fund, firemen's fund and pension fund in the
same manner as if the fire fighting or fire pre
vention work had been within the corporate limits of
the municipality or fire protection district.

classification.title: An Act to Amend Chapter 157 of the Acts of the General Assembly of 1938, Relating to the Issuance of Bonds by Cities.
classification.summary: This statute amends a previous act concerning the issuance of bonds by cities. It outlines the process for a city to incur indebtedness and issue bonds, including the requirement for an ordinance specifying the amount, purpose, and annual taxation for an interest and sinking fund. The ordinance must be published, and an election must be held to approve the indebtedness. The statute details the procedures for the election, including ballot printing, voting, canvassing, and certification of results. If two-thirds of the qualified voters voting on the question approve, the city legislative body may incur the indebtedness and issue bonds.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the process for cities to incur debt and issue bonds through an election. It specifies procedural requirements for ordinances, public notice, and voting. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute is purely financial and procedural in nature. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 129.
OH. B. 634)
AN ACT relating to narcotic drugs.
WHEREAS, the Federal Bureau of Narcotics has recommended
the following amendments to the Uniform Narcotic Drug Act
and It is the desire of the General Assembly to co-operate
with the Bureau of Narcotics and to keep the Act uniform,
NOW THEREFORE:
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Section 3716-1 of Carroll's Kentucky Stat
utes, 1936 edition, is amended to read as follows :
3716-1. Definitions.—The following words and
phrases, as used in this act, shall have the following
meanings, unless the context otherwise requires:
(1) "Person" includes any corporation, associa
tion, co-partnership, or one or more individuals.
(2) "Physician" means a person authorized by
law to practice medicine in this state and any other
person uathorized by law to treat sick and injured
human beings in this state and to use narcotic drugs
in connection with such treatment.
(3) "Dentist" means a person authorized by law
to practice dentistry in this state.
(4) "Veterinarian" means a person authorized
by law to practice veterinary medicine in this state.
(5) "Manufacturer" means a person who by
compounding, mixing, cultivating, growing, or other
process, produces or prepares narcotic drugs, but
does not include an apothecary who compounds nar
cotic drugs to be sold or dispensed on prescriptions.

CHAPTER 134 535
amending section 779a-2 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936
edition.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Section 7V2 of Chapter 7 of the Acts of
1938, compiled as section 3766a-22 of Baldwin's
Kentucky Statute Service, May, 1938, is amended
to read as follows:
(1) Nothing in sections 3766a-15 to 3766a-21
shall prevent the Governor from appointing, and he
may appoint, special local peace officers, for such
time as he deems necessary, to preserve the peace,
patrol, protect and preserve the property of any
person from waste or destruction. Upon the ap
plication of an owner of property for such services,
and upon payment of the fee hereinafter prescribed,
the Governor shall appoint immediately the person
recommended by the owner. No person shall be
eligible for appointment under this section unless
he has established to the satisfaction of the Gover
nor that he possesses the qualifications prescribed
by law for non-elective peace officers. The duties
of the officer shall be confined to the premises of
the property to be protected, except while in pursuit
of a person fleeing from the property after com
mitting an act of violence or destruction of the
property. In that case, the officer may pursue the
person and make arrest anywhere within this state.
When application is made for the appointment of
an officer under this section, the Governor shall
require the owner to pay into the State Treasury
an amount to equal the cost of the officer, and the
salary of the officer shall be paid direct out of the
State Treasury by order of the Governor. The
Governor may remove any officer so appointed at
will or at the request of the owner of the property.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 582

classification.title: An Act amending section 779a-2 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edition.
classification.summary: This act amends a previous statute concerning the appointment of special local peace officers. It allows the Governor to appoint such officers upon application by a property owner and payment of a fee. The officers' duties are generally confined to the property, but they can pursue and arrest individuals who flee after committing violence or destruction on the property. The owner is required to pay the cost of the officer, and the salary is paid from the State Treasury. The Governor can remove these officers at will or at the owner's request.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Section 1366 of Carroll's Kentucky Stat
utes, 1936 Edition, is hereby amended to read as
follows :
Section 1366. Every person who promises, of
fers, gives or causes or procures to be promised, of
fered or given any money or other thing of value
or makes or tenders any contract, undertaking, obli
gation, gratuity or security, for the payment of
money or for the delivery or conveyance of any
thing of value to any member of either house of the
General Assembly, or to any judicial, executive or
ministerial officer of the' Commonwealth of Ken
tucky, or to any county or city executive, ministerial,
judicial or legislative officer, including members of
boards of education and sub-district. trustees, after
such member or officer shall have been elected, and
before or after such member or officer has been
qualified and assumed office, or taken his seat, with
intent to influence his vote or decision on any ques
tion, matter, cause or proceeding which may be at
any time pending in either house of the General
Assembly or before such judicial, executive or legis
lative officer, or which may be pending before any
committee of either house of the General Assembly,
shall, on conviction, be punished by a fine of not less
than fifty dollars ($50.00) nor more than one thou
sand dollars ($1,000.00), or by imprisonment in the
state penitentiary for a period of not less than one
nor more than five years, or by both such fine and
imprisonment.

It specifies penalties for offering or giving money or anything of value to any member of the General Assembly or any judicial, executive, or ministerial officer (including county, city, and education officials) with the intent to influence their vote or decision on any matter pending before them. Penalties include fines and/or imprisonment.

Any member of the General Assembly or any other
executive, judicial, ministerial or legislative officer
of the Commonwealth of Kentucky or of any county
or city, including members of boards of education
and subdistrict trustees, who shall take or agree
to take any bribe to do or omit to do any act in his

Every prosecution under this Act must be begun
in five years from the date of the commission of the

that any teacher or other person who gives to any
sub-district trustee any remuneration in return for
recommendation or appointment to any position in
the school system shall be exempt from prosecution
under this Act if said teacher or other person ap
pears as a witness against the sub-district trustee.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Cities of all classes of the Commonwealth
may acquire by purchase, lease, donation, agreement
or otherwise lands desired and suitable for park
and recreation purposes at any place reasonably
accessible to the inhabitants of the city and either
within or without the boundaries of the city or the
county in which the city is located.
§ 2. Any city desiring to acquire lands for such
purposes may by ordinance provide for the acquisi
tion thereof and for the creation of a commission to
operate, improve, maintain and police the area and
to conduct programs thereon.
§ 3. The city may provide funds for the mainte
nance, operation and improvement of any such area
by the levy of a tax or by appropriation from the
general fund of the city and may accept donations
or grants from private persons or governmental
agencies for such purposes.
§ 4. The city may by ordinance prescribe ad
missions, fees, rentals, concessions and other
charges for the use of any such area or any of the
facilities thereof or may delegate the power to so
prescribe to the Commission.
§ 5. Any commission created under this Act to
operate, maintain, improve and police any such area
shall consist of three persons to be appointed by the

sors are appointed, except that the members first

CHAPTER 149
(H. B. 104)
AN ACT requiring serological blood tests for syphilis on pregnant
women; providing for its administration; and prescribing pen
alties for the violation thereof.
WHEREAS syphilis has been found to be the cause of many still
births, abortions and premature labors in Kentucky, and
WHEREAS, it has been found to be a frequent cause of death in
the early months of life in inherited syphilis, and
WHEREAS, it has been demonstrated that the treatment of
pregnant women afflicted with syphilis during their pregnancy
prevents the transmission of the disease to 'their off-spring:
Now, therefore,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Every physician and surgeon, or other
persons, legally permitted to engage in attendance
upon a pregnant woman in Kentucky, shall take
or cause to be taken a specimen of blood, for sero
logical test for syphilis, from such woman, as
soon as he or she is engaged to attend the woman
and has reasonable grounds for suspecting that
pregnancy exists. In case the woman is in a stage
of labor at the time the diagnosis of pregnancy is
made, which may make it inadvisable to obtain
a blood specimen then, the specimen shall be obtained
within ten days after delivery. The specimen of
blood shall be submitted to an approved laboratory
for serological test for syphilis. The laboratory
test required by this Act shall be a serological test
for syphilis approved by the State Department of
Health of Kentucky and made by the laboratory of
the State Department of Health of Kentucky, or a
laboratory approved by the State Department of
Health of Kentucky. Such laboratory test, if made
by the laboratory of the State Department of Health
of Kentucky, shall be made without charge.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 642

CHAPTER 157 625
§ 6. Right of Appeal. —An applicant or a re
cipient of Aid to the Needy Blind as provided for
in this Act, who shall be dissatisfied with the decision
on his application, or because of delayed action on
his application, for such Aid to the Needy Blind
or the amount of Aid to the Needy Blind granted
to him shall have the right to appeal to the State
Department in the manner and form prescribed by
rules and regulations of the State Department, and
the State Department shall establish rules and regu
lations which shall provide that on receipt of such
an appeal the applicant or recipient shall be given
reasonable notice and opportunity for a fair hearing.
The decision of the Department of "Welfare shall
be final.
§ 7. Aid to the Needy Blind Not Assignable.—
All Aid to the Needy Blind granted under this Act
shall be inalienable by any assignment or transfer
and shall be exempt from levy or execution under
the laws of the State.
§ 8. Short Title. —This Act shall be known as
the Aid to the Needy Blind Act.
§ 9. Repeal of Existing Laws. —All laws and
parts of laws in conflict with any of the provisions
of this Act are hereby expressly repealed.
§ 10. Separability Clause. —It is hereby de
clared to be the intent of the Legislature that if this
Act can not take effect in its entirety because any
section, paragraph, clause, or word, of this Act
shall be adjudged to be unconstitutional, such
adjudication shall not affect the remaining portion
of this Act and said remaining portion shall be
given full force and effect as completely as if the
part held unconstitutional had not been included.
Approved March 8, 1940.

Section 7. Old Age Assistance not assignable. —
All Old Age ASsistance granted under this Act
shall be inalienable by any assignment or transfer
and shall be exempt from levy or execution under
the laws of the State.

acknowledgment of any person when carrying out
the provisions of this Act and when needed for the
administration of this Act.

Admission of Patients Returned from other
States. —Legal residents of the State of Kentucky
who have become public charges in other states,
and returned to Kentucky by those states because
they have been adjudged insane or feeble-minded
by the courts of the state returning them, may be
immediately admitted to a Kentucky State Hospital
or to the Feeble-Minded Institute at the request
of any health officer. However, if a health officer
is not available for the purpose, the superintendent
of the Kentucky State institution may receive such
returned legal resident of Kentucky directly from
the hands of the out-of-state official who has him in
custody. Immediately upon such admission, the
superintendent shall notify the Circuit Court, or
the County Court if the Circuit Court is not in
session, accompanying such notification with a
petition for a lunacy inquest, but holding the re
turned legal resident in his custody until such inquest
is ordered by the court; provided that no super
intendent may receive such a person from the hands
of the officials of another state until he has been
notified by the Director of the Division of Hospitals
and Mental Hygiene that the person's legal res
idence has been investigated and verified by the
county judge, or his officially designated agent, of
the county in which residence is claimed.
Admission of Epileptics, Idiots and Feeble-
Minded. —Hereafter no epileptic, idiot, or feeble
minded person shall be committed to a State Hospi
tal for the insane. But any epileptic or feeble-minded
person becoming insane may be committed as an
insane person to a State Hospital for custody and
treatment therein. Approved by the Governor March 15, 1940

Reform included) is reported by the warden of that

of the Division of Hospitals and Mental Hygiene to

mate, such examination to be conducted either by

for that purpose. If this examination reveals that
the inmate is in need of observation and treatment
on account of mental deficiency or mental illness,
and that such observation and treatment can not
be properly carried out in the institution in which
he is incarcerated, the Director of the Division of
Hospitals and Mental Hygiene may then order his
transfer to a State hospital or institution for feeble
minded, where he shall remain until the superin
tendent of the hospital which received him advises the
Director of the Division of Hospitals and Mental

ment shall not be made and the previous action of
the Common Council or Board of Commissioners
shall be null and void and of no effect.
It is not the intent of this act to repeal any existing
act, but this act shall constitute an alternative
method of providing for sewer construction, ex
tension, and improvement.
WHEREAS, many of the cities and towns of Ken
tucky, to which this act is applicable are now without
adequate sewers, and other necessary appurtenances
to sewer systems, this endangering the health and
convenience of the inhabitants thereof, and whereas,
the Works Projects Administration is offering to
extend its facilities to aid in the construction of
such sewers, and other necessary appurtenances to
sewer systems, it is necessary that said cities and
towns be equipped to take advantage of such offer
at once;
THEREFORE, an emergency is declared to exist
and this act shall take effect immediately upon its
passage and approval by the Governor.
Neither approved nor vetoed.

§ 2. That section 1 of chapter 36, Acts of the

An outgoing sheriff, as soon as his successor has
been qualified and inducted into office and his
official bond approved, shall immediately vacate
his office, deliver to his successor all books, papers,
records and other property held by virtue of his
office and shall make a full and complete settlement
of his accounts as sheriff except as provided in this
section.
There is hereby created the office of special
tax collector whose sole duty it shall be to collect,
as far as he can up until the first day of June fol
lowing the expiration of the sheriff's term of office,
all unpaid tax bills which may be in possession of
the sheriff upon the date of expiration of his term
as sheriff and shall also collect the franchise tax
due and payable on or before June 1st, after his
term of office as sheriff has expired. If the out
going sheriff so desires, he may elect to act as
special tax collector for the balance of the current
tax year, in which event he shall keep all unpaid
tax bills which he may have in his possession on
the date of expiration of his term as sheriff and
shall collect and account for same as provided by
law, and he shall have until the first day of June
after his term of office as sheriff has expired to
make a settlement for all charges of taxes made
against him and for all tax monies received by
him, both as sheriff and as special tax collector,
with the Department of Revenue and the fiscal court
of his county and to receive his quietus, and im
mediately thereafter he shall deliver these records
to the incumbent sheriff. It is further provided

CHAPTER 167

AN ACT amending and re-enacting Section 4072, of Kentucky
Statutes, Carroll's edition, 1938, relating to advancement of
money to defray expenses and partial payment on salaries
of the county tax commissioner in counties containing a city
of the first class, and repealing conflicting laws.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
That Section 4072, of the Kentucky Statutes, Car
roll's edition 1938, relating to county tax commis
sioners in counties containing cities of the first
class, be amended by striking therefrom, after the
words "advancement of" the words Five Thousand
Five Hundred Dollars ($5,500.00) and inserting in
lieu thereof the words Six Thousand Dpllars
($6,000.00) so that the said Section 4072 as amended
will read, as follows, to-wit:
1. In counties containing cities of the first class,
the county tax commissioner shall be entitled to
receive an advancement of Six Thousand Dollars
($6,000.00) per month to defray necessary official
expenses and partial payment upon the salaries of
himself and his deputies and assistants, and the
Department of Finance shall, on the first of each
calendar month draw a warrant on the Treasurer
in favor of such county tax commissioner for such
sum. The sum of such advancements shall be
deducted from the total amount found to be due
such tax commissioner when yearly settlements are
made. Should the tax commissioner in such county
die, resign or be removed from office, or should
the office of county tax commissioner in such county
for any cause become vacant, the sums advanced
hereunder, shall be deducted from the total sum due
for making assessments.

tucky without having first paid the fee and obtained

cent interest until paid.

demeanor and the person so violating it shall be

Five Hundred Dollars ($500) and/or imprisoned

stitute legal proceedings for the purpose of

CHAPTER 169
(H. B. 88.)
AN ACT to amend and re-enact section 3181 of Carroll's Ken
tucky Statutes, 1936 edition.
Be it Enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. That Section 3181 of Carroll's Kentucky
Statutes, 1936 Edition, be, and the same hereby is,
amended and re-enacted so that when the same is
so amended and re-enacted it shall read as follows :
"Section 3181. Board of equalization; qualifica
tions; compensation; chairman and clerk; general
powers and duties. —There shall be a board of equal
ization to consist of three persons, who shall be
selected by the board of commissioners. No person
shall be selected as a member of said board who
shall not at the time be a housekeeper and owner of
real estate of the city, and shall not have been a
resident thereof five years next preceding his
election. Said board shall be paid such compensa
tion as may, by ordinance, be fixed, and shall meet
at a suitable place to be provided by the city on the
first Monday in January of each year. They shall
first be duly sworn faithfully to discharge their
duties, shall elect from among themselves a chair
man and a clerk and shall then notify the commis
sioner of public finance that they are ready to
receive the assessment books, etc., which the com
missioner of public finance shall deliver them, tak
ing his receipt therefor. The assessor shall be in
constant attendance on said board and shall furnish
them all information he can. They shall have power
to cause all city records to be brought to them for
their inspection by the custodian thereof, and if
it be necessary to retain them for defense, may do
so by receipting therefor to the custodian thereof.

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact section 3181 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 edition.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 3181 of Carroll's Kentucky Statutes, 1936 Edition, concerning the qualifications, compensation, and duties of a city board of equalization. The board will consist of three persons selected by the board of commissioners, who must be housekeepers and owners of real estate in the city, and residents for five years preceding their election. The board will meet annually, elect a chairman and clerk, and receive assessment books from the commissioner of public finance. The assessor will attend the board, and the board has the power to inspect city records.

It outlines qualifications for board members such as being a housekeeper and property owner, and a residency requirement. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation, and repealing, amend
ing and re-enacting section one (1) of Chapter 63 of the Acts
of the 1938 Regular Session of the General Assembly.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Definitions. —Whenever used in this Act
unless the context requires otherwise
1. "Person" shall mean and include any in
dividual, firm, partnership, association, corporation
or other group or combination acting as a unit.
2. "Clerk" shall mean the clerk of the county
court in the county where the business is to be
conducted or the privilege exercised.
3. "Department" shall mean the Department of
Revenue.
§ 2. Issued by Clerk.—All licenses shall be
granted by the clerk of the county court of the
county in which the business is proposed to be
conducted and shall be issued in writing and on
forms to be prepared by the Department of Revenue
and furnished to each county court clerk from time
to time as may be necessary for use in his office.
§ 3. Expiration Date of Licenses; Abatement of
Fees for Fractional Part of Fiscal Year.—All
licenses to be issued under this Act shall be obtained
and the license tax shall become due and payable
on July first of each year and shall expire on June
thirtieth of each year. When any person shall apply
for any license after July first of any year, he shall
be charged an amount equal to as many twelfths of
the annual license tax as there are calendar months
remaining (including the month in which the license
is granted) until the following July first.

CHAPTER 174 673
such monopolies, trusts, or combinations in the interest of the
general welfare, by authority of the general police power as
well as to fulfill the mandatory duty imposed by section 198
of the Constitution of Kentucky.
WHEREAS, chain stores or multiple mercantile establishments
enjoy certain specific advantages not common to independent
merchants such as: centralized warehousing of goods or distri
bution from a single warehouse to numerous stores; pooling
of buying so as to obtain price advantages or more favorable
cash discounts or rebates; transferring goods from a store
where the market is stagnant to one that is active; and cer
tain other general advantages in buying, selling, advertising,
accounting, supervision and management;
WHEREAS, because of the very nature of chain organizations
and the advantages peculiar to them, a single unit or store in
the chain may sell merchandise at less than cost and let the
other links in the chain absorb the loss; and thereby force an
independent merchant out of business so that virtually the
entire business of a small community may be eventually taken
over by the chain ;
WHEREAS, chain stores or merchantile establishments do run
price leaders at a price below cost or the real value of the
merchandise in contravention of section 198 of the Constitu
tion of this Commonwealth;
WHEREAS, the advantages and practices aforementioned among
others tend strongly to create monopolies and restrain or limit
trade;
WHEREAS, said advantages do and said practices may become
more pronounced as the number of stores or mercantile estab
lishments in the chain increases; and the profits per store and
net income on sales and profits from the investment tend to
increase as the number of stores or mercantile establishments
in the chain increases, while the relationship of salaries and
wages to the cost of goods sold decreases as the number of
stores or mecantile establishments in a chain increases;
WHEREAS, the stability of the local communities is important to
the welfare of the state, and small merchants are essential to
that stability in that they necessarily maintain an intimate
business relationship with local bankers, wholesalers, me
chanics, artisians and farmers and take a greater interest in
local charitable, religious, educational, cultural and civic af
fairs and thereby use their influences and resources for the
purpose of creating a higher degree of loyalty to their coin-

CHAPTER 175
(H. B. 167)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation and prescribing certain
duties, powers, and penalties relating to the listing, assess
ment, and taxation of certain intangible property.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Any assessment of accounts receivable and
notes which were listed with the county tax com
missioner or the county board of supervisors shall
be subject to being reopened by the Department
of Revenue within three years after the date as of
which they were assessed, unless the assessed value
thereof is the face value or has been established by
a court of competent jurisdiction.

If upon reopening such assessment the Depart

creased, it shall give notice thereof to the taxpayer,

partment of Revenue shall certify it to the ap
propriate county court clerk for the preparation of

same manner and subject to the same conditions as
in the case of omitted tax bills. Such tax bill shall
be handled and collected as an omitted tax bill, and
if issued on or after the date on which the tax would
have become delinquent had the property been
properly and timely assessed by the county tax
commissioner and the county board of supervisors,
it shall include interest at the rate of one-half of one
per cent (1/2 of 1%) per month or fractional part
thereof, computed from such date and shall become
due and payable upon notice to the taxpayer thereof
and shall be delinquent sixty (60) days from the
date of such notice. Upon becoming delinquent the
total amount of such tax bill shall bear interest at
the rate of one-half of one per cent (% of 1%) a
month or fractional part thereof, and penalty of
six per cent (6%) of the total amount of the bill
shall be paid.

CHAPTER 176
(H. B. 248)
AN ACT relating to revenue and taxation, and to tax the gross
amount of bank night awards.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Definitions — (a) "Bank night awards" shall
mean and include any gift, prize, or award of cash
which is given by lot, drawing or other method by
places of amusement and/or entertainment or per
sons operating the same.
(b) "Places of amusement" when used in this
Act shall mean places of amusement and/or enter
tainment as defined in section 1, chapter 34, of the
Acts of the 1938 regular session of the General
Assembly.
(c) "Person" shall mean and include indi
viduals, firms, joint stock companies, syndicates and
corporations.
(d) "Proprietor" shall mean and include any
person or persons who owns, operates, or manages
a place of amusement and/or entertainment.

4189-6. Method of assessment; notice; time of

additional information it may secure, to ascertain
and fix the fair value of that part of the capital
stock of such corporations upon which the license
tax herein provided shall be based. The Depart
ment of Revenue in arriving at the value of the

value ; and may in any case require such additional
information as it deems necessary to enable it to

duty of the Department of Revenue to notify every
such corporation of the amount so assessed, by plac
ing the notice of assessment in the United States
mails, and the corporation shall have thirty days
from the date of notice of assessment in which to
pay the tax to the Department of Revenue, Frank
fort, Kentucky, by check or money order payable
to the Kentucky State Treasurer, or to protest the
assessment to the Department of Revenue. After
the protest has been acted upon by the Department
of Revenue the corporation protesting shall have
fifteen days in which to pay the tax found to be due
or to appeal as provided for in sections 5 and 5a of
Chapter 4 of the Acts of the first
session of the 1938 General Assembly.

§ 7. A new section of Carroll's Kentucky

4189-7. First report on fiscal year basis; change
in reporting period.— (1) The first report to bo filed
by a corporation operating upon a fiscal year
accounting basis shall be filed on or before the first
filing date after the effective date of this Act. This
return shall cover the operations for the entire year
preceding the close of the fiscal year accounting
period; and the assessment of value shall be com
puted in the same manner as on any other return ;
however, the tax due shall be computed on that pro
portionate part of the year that the period from
January 1, 1940, to the close of the fiscal year, bears
to an entire twelve month period.
(2) A corporation may change its reporting
period from a calendar year to a fiscal year or from
a fiscal year to a calendar year by securing written
permission from the Department of Revenue. If a
corporation so changes its basis of reporting, the
first report filed on the new filing date shall cover
operations for the period between the close of the
old accounting period and the close of the new
accounting period. The assessment of value shall
be computed in the same manner as on any other
return; however, the tax due shall be computed on
that proportionate part of the assessment that the
period between the close of the old accounting
period and the close of the new accounting period
bears to the entire twelve month period.

It specifies requirements for newspaper publication or posting of notices, including the information to be included, the timing of these notices, and the placement of a copy at the courthouse door. It also details the process for mailing notices to delinquent taxpayers and outlines the compensation for sheriffs or collectors for their services in advertising and collecting these taxes. The fees are to be added to the tax claim and paid by the delinquent taxpayer, not by the state or local taxing districts.
classification.reasoning: The statute details the administrative and financial procedures for handling delinquent tax sales. It does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. The focus is purely on the legal and procedural aspects of tax collection and sale of property for unpaid taxes. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

delinquency.

classification.title: CHAPTER 180
classification.summary: This statute outlines procedures for appealing judgments related to tax assessments and the refund process for voided certificates of delinquency. It grants appellate jurisdiction to the circuit court and the Court of Appeals of Kentucky for tax-related judgments. It also details the refund process for individuals holding voided certificates of delinquency, specifying pro rata refunds from the State, county, and taxing districts, with preference given to school districts and counties unable to refund currently. The Department of Revenue is empowered to require reports, promulgate rules, and supervise the administration and enforcement of the Act, including maintaining an index of certificates of delinquency. Penalties are prescribed for willful non-compliance with rules and regulations.

The Commonwealth shall have a lien of equal
rank and dignity to the lien provided in section 2
of this Act on all property of a taxpayer who owes
any property tax, license tax, inheritance or estate
tax, excise tax, income tax, or other tax, when such
taxes are not included in or covered by the lien pro
vided in section 2 of this Act. Said lien shall accrue
at the time the liability becomes fixed. The Depart
ment of Revenue may file notice of same with the
county court clerk of any county where the taxpayer
has property, giving the name of the taxpayer, his
address, and the amount of the lien. The clerk shall
file same in the same manner as lis pendens are
filed, and the file shall be designated "Miscellaneous
State Tax Liens." The notice, when so filed, shall
be constructive notice to all persons of the lien on
the property having a legal situs in that county,
except that nothing in this Act shall be construed to
alter or change in any way the law relative to the
rights and duties of a holder in due course as pro
vided in sections 3720b-l to 3720c, Carroll's Ken
tucky Statutes, Baldwin's 1936 Revision, or affect
the rights of any person taking the property or a
lien thereon for value without actual or constructive
notice. The clerk shall be entitled to a fee of one
dollar and twenty-five cents ($1.25) for filing the
lien, and said fee shall become a part of the lien as an
added cost of the delinquent taxpayer to be paid by
him as a part of the amount necessary to release the
lien, and in no event shall the Commonwealth be
required to pay same.

that admission charges of less than eleven cents

ductions presented by a civic organization in a park

The Department of Highways shall be headed by
a Commissioner of Highways, who shall have sole
charge of the administration of the Department.
With prior approval of the Governor in writing, the
Commissioner of Highways shall appoint as State
Highway Engineer, a civil engineer who has had at
least six years executive engineering experience in
the design, supervision and construction of high
ways. The State Highway Engineer shall be paid
an annual salary of Five Thousand Dollars
($5,000.00). He shall be the technical advisor to the
Commissioner of Highways, and under the direction
of said Commissioner, he shall supervise all con
struction and maintenance work of the Department
and he shall perform such other duties as are as
signed to him by the Commissioner.
The Department shall be divided for administra
tive convenience into a division of construction, a
division of records, a division of equipment, a
division of maintenance, and a division of highway
patrol, which division shall be responsible for the
enforcement of the highway laws of the Common
wealth and for such other functions as the Governor
may direct in writing. Each division shall perform
such duties as may be assigned it by the Commis
sioner of Highways and shall be headed by a director
who shall act under the direction and supervision

mis states. This Act shall be known as "The Robinson Farm
Bureau Act".
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky:
§ 1. Any rural electric co-operative corporation
organized under a law of any state contiguous to
this state, which law is substantially similar to the
law under which such corporations may be organized
in this state, may extend its operations into this
state for a distance not exceeding three miles from
the boundary between that state and this state,
and such extension shall not be considered doing
business in this state within the meaning of the
statutes regulating or taxing foreign corporations
doing business in this state. Such corporation shall
be entitled to the same exemptions granted to, and
shall pay the same tax required of, domestic
corporations under section 27 of Chapter 6 of the
Acts of 1936, fourth extraordinary session.
§ 2. The operations of such corporation within
this state shall be subject to the supervision of the
Public Service Commission of Kentucky, and the
Commission may take the necessary action to require
the corporation to furnish adequate service at
reasonable rates. If the corporation fails to comply
with the regulations and requirements of the Com
mission it shall forfeit the privilege granted by this
Act,
§ 3. The privilege granted by this Act shall be
effective for a period of five years from the effective
date of this Act, at which date it shall expire, unless
the contiguous state grants a similar privilege to
rural electric co-operative corporations incorporated
in this state, in which case it shall continue so long
as the contiguous state continues to grant the
same privilege.
Neither approved nor vetoed.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1940, p. 780

classification.title: The Robinson Farm Bureau Act
classification.summary: This act allows rural electric co-operative corporations from contiguous states, with substantially similar laws, to extend operations into Kentucky up to three miles from the border. These extensions are not considered 'doing business' for foreign corporation regulations or taxes. The corporations are subject to the Public Service Commission's supervision and must provide adequate service at reasonable rates. The privilege is granted for five years and continues if the contiguous state offers a similar privilege to Kentucky corporations.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation and taxation of rural electric co-operative corporations operating across state borders. It does not contain any language or provisions that suggest racial discrimination or segregation. The focus is on economic and regulatory cooperation between states for utility services. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.
